These people also typically have friends and extended family members in mainland China. These connections, alongside the economic conditions brought about by China's economic reform since the late 1970s, have allowed them to support their corporate objectives on and private wealth accumulation by introducing the wholesale market of mainland Chinese products into Burma and other Southeast Asian markets. * China–Burma relations * Kokang people * Thai Chinese * Rangoon * * [https://web.archive.org/web/20060507103249/http://newidea.nandaw.net/ Newidea! Myanmar information website] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20060423014826/http://mocsa.org.tw/ Burma Overseas Chinese Student Association] * [http://www.gqb.gov.cn/ Overseas Chinese Affairs Office of the State Council of the People's Republic of China] (Chinese-language only) * [https://archive.today/20041029031455/http://163.29.16.16/english/index.asp Overseas Chinese Affairs Commission, R.O.C.] * [http://www.irrawaddy.org/res/china.html Chronology of Chinese-Burmese Relations] of The Irrawaddy * [https://reader.library.cornell.edu/docviewer/digital?id=sea:362#page/316/mode/1up/ Twentieth century impressions of Burma : its history, people, commerce, industries, and resources] * [https://yeocheowkaw.org/yeo-cheow-kaw-and-rangoon-kian-teik-tong-2/ Yeo Cheow Kaw and Rangoon Kian Teik Tong] * [https://books.google.com/books?id=v9QEBAAAQBAJ&dq=huang+daniel+patrick+lim&pg=RA1-PA45 Southeast Asian Personalities of Chinese Descent: Biographical dictionary] * [https://books.google.com/books?id=fZo7DgAAQBAJ&q=Keng+Beng+Chong+burma%2F&pg=PA198 Chinese in Colonial Burma: A Migrant Community in A Multiethnic State] Chinese Category:China–Myanmar relations Myanmar Myanmar *

stable.title: Tolkien and race
stable.clean_text: J. R. R. Tolkien's Middle-earth fantasy writings have been said to embody outmoded attitudes to race. He was exposed as a child to Victorian attitudes to race, and to a literary tradition of monsters. In his personal life, he was anti-racist both in peacetime and during the two World Wars. With the late 19th-century background of eugenics and a fear of moral decline, Robin Anne Reid and others have suggested that the mention of race mixing in The Lord of the Rings embodies scientific racism. David Ibata has stated that Peter Jackson's depiction of the Orcs in his The Lord of the Rings film trilogy was modelled on racist wartime propaganda caricatures of the Japanese."Not nice; very cruel wicked Men they look. Almost as bad as Orcs, and much bigger."|Gollum on the Men of Harad }} {{blockquote|He was glad that he could not see the dead face. He wondered what the man's name was and where he came from; and if he was really evil of heart, or what lies or threats had led him on the long march from his home.|Sam Gamgee, on the dead Harad warrior }} Gollum's perspective is absurd, Straubhaar says, and cannot be taken as an authority on Tolkien's opinion; Straubhaar finds Gamgee's response "harder to find fault with". Fimi says that, in the same scene, Tolkien is "far from demonising the enemy or dehumanising the 'other. Tolkien scholars have debated the relationship of Dwarves to Jews. Tolkien stated that he thought of his Dwarves as reminiscent of "the Jews", and that the Dwarves' words were "constructed to be Semitic". Zak Cramer documents in Mallorn the evidence for Jewish influence in Tolkien's Middle-earth writings. He comments that words like "Adûnaic" and "Rohirrim", and the structure of the Dwarvish language Khuzdul, are all evidently "Hebraic". Some commentators draw parallels between the Dwarves as a diasporic people yearning for an ancestral homeland and Zionism. Tolkien's portrayal of Dwarves raises the question, examined by Rebecca Brackmann in Mythlore, of whether there was an element of antisemitism, however deeply buried, in Tolkien's account of the Dwarves, inherited from English attitudes of his time. Brackmann notes that Tolkien attempted to work through the issue in his Middle-earth writings. Stuart notes Tolkien's phrase about the Jews, "that gifted people", and discusses the evidence for and against his having antisemitic views. thumb|[[Dimitra Fimi writes that Middle-earth assumes a fixed hierarchy of types of being, like the medieval great chain of being. (Ramon Llull's Ladder of Ascent and Descent of the Mind, 1305, pictured)]] Anderson Rearick III writes that in Middle-earth, darkness and black are linked with evil Orcs and the Dark Lord Sauron, and that the Orcs are essentially expendable,}} Fimi describes Tolkien's mentions of "swarthy complexions" and slanted eyes as "straight out of Victorian anthropology, which links mental qualities and physique". A variety of commentators have noted that orcs are somewhat like caricatures of non-Europeans. The editor and critic Andrew O'Hehir describes orcs as "a subhuman race bred by Morgoth and Sauron (although not created by them) that is morally irredeemable and deserves only death. They are dark-skinned and slant-eyed, and although they possess reason, speech, social organisation and, as Shippey mentions, a sort of moral sensibility, they are inherently evil." In a letter to his son, Christopher, who was serving in the Royal Air Force in the Second World War, Tolkien wrote of orcs as appearing on both sides of the conflict:}} The literary critic Jenny Turner, writing in the London Review of Books, endorses O'Hehir's comment that orcs are "by design and intention a northern European's paranoid caricature of the races he has dimly heard about". rect 100 100 250 250 The Shire rect 500 50 850 150 Tolkien's moral geography rect 150 350 450 550 Gondor rect 650 350 850 550 Mordor rect 550 550 750 650 Harad rect 10 10 900 675 commons:File:Tolkien's Moral Geography of Middle-Earth.svg }} John Magoun, writing in The J. R. R. Tolkien Encyclopedia, says that Middle-earth has a "fully expressed moral geography". The peoples of Middle-earth vary from the hobbits of the Shire in the Northwest, evil "Easterlings" in the East, and "imperial sophistication and decadence" in the South. Magoun explains that Gondor is both virtuous, being West, and has problems, being South; Mordor in the Southeast is hellish, while Harad in the extreme South "regresses into hot savagery".}} Tolkien agreed that there was a West vs East moral divide, but stated that it developed naturally over the course of the fictional history and denied that it applied to the modern world: {{blockquote|The goodness of the West and the badness of the East has no modern reference. The concept came about through the necessities of narrative. }} Scholars such as Fimi note that Tolkien was in some ways clearly anti-racist, as he actively opposed "racialist" theories, spoke out against Nazism, called Adolf Hitler a "ruddy little ignoramus", and opposed anti-German propaganda in wartime.}} He drafted two letters to Rütten & Loening; only one survives, and his biographer Humphrey Carpenter presumes that Unwin sent the other to Rütten & Loening. The surviving draft says}} Rearick states that Tolkien expressed an anti-racist position during the Second World War. Rearick writes that Tolkien reacted with anger to the excesses of anti-German propaganda during World War II. In a 1944 letter to his son Christopher, Tolkien wrote:}} Carpenter writes in his biography that Tolkien found the war distressing "almost as much for idological as for personal reasons", citing Tolkien's 1941 statement in praise of what he saw as the German virtues "of obedience and patriotism", and lamenting Hitler's ruining "that noble northern spirit[...] which I have ever loved, and tried to present in its true light". The scholar of English literature Robert Tally concurs, writing that the letter showed that "Tolkien's anger was palpable", although that in no way negated the hierarchy of races and their "intramural subdivisions or ethnicities" within Tolkien's legendarium. Commentators including the scholar of English literature Julie Pridmore have noted Tolkien's opposition to South African racism; she describes his views as "advanced in terms of the acceptance of different cultures and ethnicities". During the Second World War, Tolkien's son Christopher, training in South Africa, expressed concern about the treatment of black people at the hands of white people, and his father replied:}} Stephen Wigmore wrote in The Spectator that Tolkien explicitly rejected the South African policy of apartheid, racial segregation, in his 1959 speech on retiring as a professor at the University of Oxford. Jefferson in The New Statesman similarly wrote that Tolkien "strongly disapproved of apartheid". Discussions of race in Middle-earth have continued as filmmakers have sought to depict orcs and other races. The journalist David Ibata writes that the orcs in Peter Jackson's Tolkien films look much like "the worst depictions of the Japanese drawn by American and British illustrators during World War II." The science fiction author N. K. Jemisin writes that "Orcs are fruit of the poison vine that is human fear of 'the Other'." Tally calls the orcs a demonised enemy, despite (he writes) Tolkien's objections to the demonisation of the enemy in the two World Wars. Reviewing Robert Stuart's 2022 book Tolkien, Race, and Racism in Middle-earth, Tally writes that while the book is valuable and thorough, Stuart's "desire to absolve Tolkien ... is understandable, but it is important to recognize and to emphasize the truth lest the threats posed by white supremacists ... be too easily dismissed or underestimated." The Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power, a series about events in the Second Age long before the events in the War of the Ring, screened from September 2022, has according to Sam Thielman attracted "fierce debate" about its handling of race. This is, Thielman states, through its casting of "people of color" in roles as Dwarves, Harfoots (precursors of Hobbits) and Elves. The casting has pleased some fans but angered others who feel that the fantasy genre is and must remain white. Many of those hostile to the casting have cited a statement misattributed to Tolkien: "Evil cannot create anything new, it can only spoil and destroy".{{efn|In the chapter "The Tower of Cirith Ungol" in The Return of the King (Book 6, chapter 1), Tolkien has the character Frodo say "The Shadow that bred them can only mock, it cannot make: not real things of its own. I don't think it gave life to the orcs, it only ruined them and twisted them". A statement by a fictional character may be suggestive of the opinion of the author. See also Tolkien's sentience dilemma.}} The cast of the series have spoken out against racism aimed at the actors involved. The fantasy author Neil Gaiman, defending the casting, commented that "Tolkien described the Harfoots as "browner of skin" than the other hobbits. So I think anyone grumbling is either racist or hasn't read their Tolkien." Gaiman had earlier written in a wider context that "White Supremacists don't own Norse Mythology, even if some of them think they do". Stuart Heritage observed in The Guardian that the Harfoots in The Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power speak in Irish accents, behave as friendly peasants, and are accompanied by Celtic music; and that they resemble the 19th century caricaturist John Leech's "wildly unflattering" depictions of the Irish in Punch magazine. File:Tokio Kid Say 'Much Waste of Material Make So-o-o-0 Happy! Thank You.jpg|Peter Jackson's film versions of Tolkien's Orcs have been compared to wartime caricatures of the Japanese (here, an American propaganda poster).|alt=Poster showing a fanged caricature of "Tokio kid", a Japanese person pointing a bloody knife at a sign that reads "Much waste of material make so-o-o-o happy! Thank you!" File:Sara Zwangobani LotR-TRoP Asia Premiere 2022.jpg|The casting of "people of color" in The Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power, such as of Sara Zwangobani as a Harfoot, pleased some fans and angered others. File:John Leech's cartoon 'Justice to Ireland'.jpg|The Harfoots in The Rings of Power speak in Irish accents and have been said to resemble John Leech's Irish peasants, as in his cartoon "Justice to Ireland". * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * Category:Themes of The Lord of the Rings Category:Middle-earth themes Tolkien Category:Race-related controversies in literature Category:Anti-racism in the United Kingdom

contested.title: Cloud seeding
contested.raw_text:
{{Short description|Weather modification that condenses clouds to cause rainfall}}
{{Multiple issues| 
{{More citations needed|date=October 2024}} 
{{POV|date=October 2024}} 
}}
{{use dmy dates|date=February 2025}}
[[Image:Cloud Seeding.svg|thumb|upright=1.3|Cloud seeding can be done by ground generators or planes<!---this is cited in the body - don't need it in the caption--->]]
[[File:Cloudseedingimagecorrected.jpg|alt=Cloud Seeding|thumb|upright=1.2|This image explaining cloud seeding shows a substance – either silver iodide or dry ice – being dumped onto the cloud, which then becomes a rain shower. The process shown in the upper-right is what is happening in the cloud and the process of condensation upon the introduced material.<ref>Infographic: Naomi E Tesla; Source for image: [http://www.fletcherboland.com/photos/mountains/11.php Fletcher Boland {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303215009/http://www.fletcherboland.com/photos/mountains/11.php|date=2016-03-03}}</ref>]]
<!---see [[WP:LEADFOLLOWSBODY]]--- don't need citations in the lead - assertions need to be repeated and cited in the body.--->
'''Cloud seeding''' is a type of [[weather modification]] that aims to change the amount or type of [[precipitation]], mitigate hail, or disperse fog. The usual objective is to increase [[rain]] or [[snow]], either for its own sake or to prevent precipitation from occurring in days afterward.

[[Cloud]] seeding is undertaken by dispersing substances into the air that serve as [[Cloud condensation nuclei|cloud condensation]] or [[ice nucleus|ice nuclei]]. Common agents include [[silver iodide]], [[potassium iodide]], and dry ice, with [[hygroscopic]] materials like table salt gaining popularity due to their ability to attract moisture. Techniques vary from static seeding, which encourages ice particle formation in [[supercooled]] clouds to increase precipitation, to dynamic seeding, designed to enhance convective cloud development through the release of [[latent heat]].

Methods of dispersion include aircraft and ground-based generators or balloons, with newer approaches involving [[Unmanned aerial vehicle|drone]]s delivering [[electric charge]]s to stimulate rainfall, or infrared [[laser]] pulses aimed at inducing particle formation. Despite decades of research and application, cloud seeding's effectiveness remains a subject of debate among scientists, with studies offering mixed results on its impact on precipitation enhancement.

Environmental and health impacts are considered minimal due to the low concentrations of substances used, but concerns persist over the potential accumulation of seeding agents in sensitive ecosystems. The practice has a long history, with initial experiments dating back to the 1940s, and has been used for various purposes, including agricultural benefits, [[water supply]] augmentation, and event planning. Legal frameworks primarily focus on prohibiting the military or hostile use of weather modification techniques, leaving the ownership and regulation of cloud-seeding activities to national discretion. Despite skepticism and debate over its efficacy and environmental impact, cloud seeding continues to be explored and applied in regions worldwide as a tool for weather modification.

==Methods==
===Salts===
[[File:Ground Based Silver Iodide Generator.jpg|thumb|Ground-based silver iodide generator – [[Colorado]], US ]]
The most common chemicals used for cloud seeding include [[silver iodide]],<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/compound/Silver-Iodide|title=Silver Iodide|access-date=2022-01-26|archive-date=2022-01-26|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220126213907/https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/compound/Silver-Iodide|url-status=live}}</ref> [[potassium iodide]] and [[dry ice]] (solid carbon dioxide). [[Liquid propane]], which expands into a gas, has also been used. It can produce ice crystals at higher temperatures than silver iodide. After promising research, the use of [[hygroscopy|hygroscopic]] materials, such as [[table salt]], is becoming more popular.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Hill | first1 = S A. | last2 = Ming | first2 = Yi | year = 2012 | title = Nonlinear climate response to regional brightening of tropical marine stratocumulus | journal = Geophysical Research Letters | volume = 39 | issue = 15| page = L15707 | doi = 10.1029/2012GL052064 | bibcode=2012GeoRL..3915707H| s2cid = 128599895 | doi-access =  }}</ref>

When cloud seeding, increased snowfall takes place when temperatures within the clouds are between −20 and −7 °C (-4 °F and 20 °F).<ref name=sac>{{cite news |first=Matt |last=Hill |title=Cloud seeding, no longer magical thinking, is poised for use this winter |url=https://www.sacbee.com/news/local/article2582373.html |work=Sacramento Bee |date=2013-11-11 |access-date=2020-01-28 |archive-date=2020-03-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200305220724/https://www.sacbee.com/news/local/article2582373.html |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Freezing]] [[nucleation]] is induced by the introduction of substances similar to silver iodide, which has a [[crystalline]] structure like ice.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Vonnegut |first1=B. |last2=Chessin |first2=Henry |date=1971-11-26 |title=Ice Nucleation by Coprecipitated Silver Iodide and Silver Bromide |journal=Science |volume=174 |issue=4012 |pages=945–946 |bibcode=1971Sci...174..945V |doi=10.1126/science.174.4012.945 |issn=0036-8075 |pmid=17773193 |s2cid=37459080}}</ref>

===Electric charges===
Since 2021, the [[United Arab Emirates]] have been using drones equipped with a payload of electric-charge emission instruments and customized sensors that fly at low altitudes and deliver an electric charge to air molecules.<ref>{{cite web |title=UAE to test cloud-busting drones to boost rainfall |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-56428984 |website=BBC |access-date=25 July 2021 |date=17 March 2021 |archive-date=24 July 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210724130730/https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-56428984 |url-status=live }}</ref> This method may have contributed to a significant rainstorm in July 2021.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Doliner |first1=Anabelle  |title=Dubai Creates Fake Rain Using Drones to Battle 122 Degree Heat |url=https://www.newsweek.com/dubai-creates-fake-rain-using-drones-battle-122-degree-heat-1611887 |access-date=25 July 2021 |agency=Newskeek |date=21 July 2021 |archive-date=24 July 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210724132553/https://www.newsweek.com/dubai-creates-fake-rain-using-drones-battle-122-degree-heat-1611887 |url-status=live }}</ref> For instance, in [[Al Ain]] it rained 6.9 millimeters (¼ inch) on 20–21 July.<ref>{{cite web |title=Al Ain Historical Weather |url=https://www.worldweatheronline.com/lang/en/al-ain-weather-history/abu-dhabi/ae.aspx |website=World Weather |access-date=25 July 2021 |archive-date=25 July 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210725224859/https://www.worldweatheronline.com/lang/en/al-ain-weather-history/abu-dhabi/ae.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref>

===Infrared laser pulses===
{{Further|Laser-assisted water condensation}}
An electronic mechanism was tested in 2010, when [[infrared]] [[laser]] pulses were directed to the air above [[Berlin]] by researchers from the [[University of Geneva]].<ref name=ns>{{cite magazine|title=Laser creates clouds over Germany|url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/dn18848-laser-creates-clouds-over-germany.html|magazine=[[New Scientist]]|date=2010-05-02|access-date=2010-11-21|archive-date=2010-12-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101205211220/http://www.newscientist.com/article/dn18848-laser-creates-clouds-over-germany.html|url-status=live}}</ref> The experimenters posited that the pulses would encourage atmospheric [[sulfur dioxide]] and [[nitrogen dioxide]] to form particles that would then act as seeds.<ref name=ns/>

==Effectiveness==
Despite decades of research and application, cloud seeding's effectiveness remains a subject of debate among scientists, with studies offering mixed results on its impact on precipitation enhancement, according to a report issued by the US [[Government Accountability Office]] in December 2024.<ref name=gao>{{cite web | title=Cloud Seeding Technology: Assessing Effectiveness and Other Challenges | website=U.S. GAO | date=19 December 2024 | url=https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-25-107328 | access-date=16 February 2025}}</ref> Whether cloud seeding is effective in producing a statistically significant increase in precipitation has been a matter of academic debate, with contrasting results depending on the study in question and contrasting opinion among experts.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Moseman|first1=Andrew|title=Does cloud seeding work?|url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/cloud-seeding-china-snow/|access-date=10 November 2016|work=Scientific American|date=19 February 2009|archive-date=10 February 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210210170533/https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/cloud-seeding-china-snow/|url-status=live}}</ref>

A study conducted by the United States [[National Academy of Sciences]] failed to find statistically significant support for cloud seeding's effectiveness. Based on its findings, Stanford University ecologist Jerry Bradley said: "I think you can squeeze out a little more snow or rain in some places under some conditions, but that's quite different from a program claiming to reliably increase precipitation." Data similar to that of the NAS study was acquired in a separate study conducted by the Wyoming Weather Modification Pilot Project, but whereas the NAS study concluded that "it is difficult to show clearly that cloud seeding has a very large effect", the WWMPP study concluded that "seeding could augment the snowpack by a maximum of 3% over an entire season."<ref name="pelley">{{cite journal|last1=Pelley|first1=Janet|title=Does cloud seeding really work?|journal=Chemical and Engineering News|date=30 May 2016|volume=94|issue=22|pages=18–21|url=http://cen.acs.org/articles/94/i22/Does-cloud-seeding-really-work.html|access-date=10 November 2016|archive-date=10 November 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161110235130/http://cen.acs.org/articles/94/i22/Does-cloud-seeding-really-work.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

In 2003, the [[National Research Council (United States)|US National Research Council]] (NRC) released a report stating, "science is unable to say with assurance which, if any, seeding techniques produce positive effects. In the 55 years following the first cloud-seeding demonstrations, substantial progress has been made in understanding the natural processes that account for our daily weather. Yet scientifically acceptable proof for significant seeding effects has not been achieved".<ref name="NRC-2003">{{cite book|title=Critical Issues in Weather Modification Research|date=2003|publisher=Committee on the Status and Future Directions in U.S Weather Modification Research and Operations; Board on Atmospheric Sciences and Climate; Division on Earth and Life Studies; National Research Council|doi=10.17226/10829|isbn=978-0-309-09053-7|edition=Paper|bibcode=2003nap..book10829N |url=https://www.nap.edu/catalog/10829/critical-issues-in-weather-modification-research|access-date=23 October 2016|archive-date=23 October 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161023210050/https://www.nap.edu/catalog/10829/critical-issues-in-weather-modification-research|url-status=live}}</ref>{{RP|13}}

A 2010 [[Tel Aviv University]] study claimed that the common practice of cloud seeding to improve rainfall, with materials such as [[silver iodide]] and frozen carbon dioxide, seems to have little if any impact on the amount of precipitation.<ref>{{cite news|last1=American Friends of Tel Aviv University|title=Cloud seeding' not effective at producing rain as once thought, new research shows|url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2010/11/101101125949.htm|access-date=23 October 2016|work=Science Daily|date=1 November 2010|archive-date=23 October 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161023201404/https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2010/11/101101125949.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> A 2011 study suggested that airplanes may produce ice particles by freezing cloud droplets that cool as they flow around the tips of propellers, over wings or over jet aircraft, and thereby unintentionally seed clouds. This could have potentially serious consequences for particular [[hail stone]] formation.<ref>[https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/07/110701121623.htm Big Hole Filled in Cloud Research] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180730080505/https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/07/110701121623.htm |date=2018-07-30 }} 1 July 2011 ''www.sciencedaily.com'', accessed 30 January 2021</ref>

In 2016, Jeff Tilley, director of weather modification at the [[Desert Research Institute]] in Reno, claimed that new technology and research has produced reliable results that make cloud seeding a dependable and affordable water supply practice for many regions.<ref name=sacbee>[https://www.sacbee.com/news/local/article2582373.html "Cloud seeding, no longer magical thinking, is poised for use this winter"] , ''Sacramento Bee'', Nov. 11, 2013</ref>{{Failed verification|date=April 2026}} Moreover, in 1998 the American Meteorological Society held that "precipitation from supercooled orographic clouds (clouds that develop over mountains) has been seasonally increased by about 10%."<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.ametsoc.org/policy/wxmod98.html |title=Planned and Inadvertent Weather Modification |website=www.ametsoc.org |access-date=2010-01-31 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100612213920/http://ametsoc.org/policy/wxmod98.html |archive-date=2010-06-12 }}</ref>

Despite the mixed scientific results, cloud seeding was attempted during the [[2008 Summer Olympics]] in Beijing to coax rain showers out of clouds before they reached the city in order to prevent rain during the [[2008 Summer Olympics opening ceremony|opening]] and [[2008 Summer Olympics closing ceremony|closing]] ceremonies.<ref>{{cite news|title=The rocket that stops the rain|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/today/hi/today/newsid_7555000/7555737.stm|access-date=23 October 2016|work=BBC Today|date=12 August 2008|archive-date=9 March 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170309230116/http://news.bbc.co.uk/today/hi/today/newsid_7555000/7555737.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> Whether this attempt was successful is a matter of dispute, with Roelof Bruintjes, who leads the National Center for Atmospheric Research's weather-modification group, remarking, "we cannot make clouds or chase clouds away".<ref>{{cite news|last1=Lipsher|first1=Steve|title=Scientist's aim: Alter weather|url=http://www.denverpost.com/2008/04/22/scientists-aim-alter-weather/|access-date=23 October 2016|work=Denver Post|date=7 May 2016|archive-date=23 October 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161023202950/http://www.denverpost.com/2008/04/22/scientists-aim-alter-weather/|url-status=live}}</ref>

==Impact on environment and health==
With an [[NFPA 704]] health hazard rating of 2, [[silver iodide]] can cause temporary incapacitation or possible residual injury to humans and other mammals with intense or chronic exposure. But several detailed ecological studies have shown negligible environmental and health impacts.<ref>Bureau of Reclamation, 1977: Project Skywater, A program of Research in Precipitation Management.  However, some research indicates that silver toxicity is bio-accumulative in aquatic environments, causing respiratory distress to some species of fish (Aquatic Toxicology Volume 49, Issues 1–2, May 2000, pp 111–129). Final Environmental Statement (INT FES 77-39).</ref><ref>Harris, Edward R., 1981: Sierra Cooperative Pilot Project – Environmental Assessment and Finding of No Significant Impact.  US Department of the Interior, Bureau of Reclamation, Denver, CO, 208 pp.</ref><ref>Howell, Wallace E., 1977: Environmental Impacts of Precipitation Management: Results and Inferences from Project Skywater. Bull. American Meteorological Society, 58, 488–501</ref> The toxicity of silver and silver compounds (from silver iodide) was shown to be of low order in some studies. These findings likely result from the minute amounts of silver generated by cloud seeding, which are about one percent of industry emissions into the atmosphere in many parts of the world, or individual exposure from tooth fillings.<ref>Steinhoff, Harold W., and Jack D. Ives, Eds., 1976: Ecological Impacts of Snowpack Augmentation in the San Juan Mountains, Colorado.  Final Report to the Bureau of Reclamation, 489 pp.</ref>

Accumulations in the soil, vegetation, and [[surface runoff]] have not been large enough to measure above natural background.<ref>Donald A. Klein, 1978: Environmental Impacts of Artificial Ice Nucleating Agents, Dowden, Hutchinson & Ross, Inc., Stroudsburg, 256 pp.</ref> A 1995 environmental assessment in the Sierra Nevada of California<ref>Parsons Engineering Science, Inc., 1995: Environmental Assessment for the Pacific Gas and Electric Company and the US Forest Service, Stanislaus National Forest.</ref> and a 2004 independent panel of experts in Australia confirmed these earlier findings.<ref>[http://www.snowyhydro.com.au/files/SPET.pdf executive summary of the research] ''www.snowyhydro.com.au'' {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080722004752/http://www.snowyhydro.com.au/files/SPET.pdf |date=July 22, 2008 }}</ref>

"In 1978, an estimated 3,000 [[tonne]]s of silver were released into the US environment. This led the US Health Services and EPA to conduct studies regarding the potential for environmental and human health hazards related to silver. These agencies and other state agencies applied the Clean Water Act of 1977 and 1987 to establish regulations on this type of pollution."<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20150227200905/https://www.weathermodification.org/images/AGI_toxicity.pdf "AGI_toxicity.pdf"], ''Weather Modification Association''</ref>

Cloud seeding over [[Kosciuszko National Park]]{{snd}}a biosphere reserve{{snd}}is problematic in that several rapid changes of environmental legislation were made to enable the trial.{{citation needed|date=September 2025}} Environmentalists{{who|date=September 2025}} are concerned about the uptake of elemental silver in a highly sensitive environment affecting the [[pygmy possum]], among other species, as well as recent high-level algal blooms in once pristine glacial lakes.{{citation needed|date=September 2025}} Research 50 years ago{{when|date=September 2025}} and analysis by the former Snowy Mountains Authority led to the cessation of the cloud seeding program in the 1950s with non-definitive results.{{citation needed|date=September 2025}} Formerly, cloud seeding was rejected in Australia on environmental grounds because of concerns about the pygmy possum.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.colongwilderness.org.au/RedIndex/NSW/Jagu99.htm |title=Jagungal|access-date=July 23, 2009 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090912023409/http://www.colongwilderness.org.au/RedIndex/NSW/Jagu99.htm |archive-date=September 12, 2009 }}</ref> The claims of negative environmental impact are disputed by peer-reviewed research, as summarized by the International Weather Modification Association.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.weathermodification.org/AGI_toxicity.pdf |access-date=September 11, 2009 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100401011120/http://www.weathermodification.org/AGI_toxicity.pdf|title=Weather Modification Association (WMA) Position on the Environmental Impact of using Silver Iodide as a Cloud Seeding Agent  |archive-date=April 1, 2010 }}</ref>

==History==
[[File:The Pittsfield Flight.png|thumb|The Pittsfield Flight; the world's first successful weather modification experiment]]
[[File:Beechcraft AT-11, Cuban Air Force (51674908619).jpg|thumb|[[Beechcraft Model 18|Beech 18]] used dry ice for cloud seeding in the early postwar era US]]
[[File:CSIRO Cloud Seeding from Wagga Airport 1958.jpg|thumb|[[Lockheed Model 18 Lodestar|Lockheed 18]] cloud seeding from Wagga Airport in 1958 [[Australia]] ]]
[[File:North American T-28A rain maker (5191517744).jpg|thumb|[[North American T-28 Trojan|T-28A Trojan]] rain maker 1960 US]]
[[File:P-40N N1223N rain maker (4806265198).jpg|thumb|[[Curtiss P-40 Warhawk|Curtiss P-40]] rain maker 1964 US, now at [[Yanks Air Museum]]]]
[[File:Curtiss P-40N N1232N (5437402621).jpg|thumb|Curtiss P-40N N1232N Weather Modification Company - cloud seeding]]
[[File:MK 2 HARVARDS HAIL SUPPRESSION CALGARY 1964.jpg|thumb|[[North American T-6 Texan|Harvard]] hail defence 1964 [[Canada]]]]
[[Image:Cessna 210 Hagelflieger Detail.jpg|thumb|[[Cessna 210]] 2006 [[Austria]]]]
[[File:4X-CBT LLBG 10-04-2015a.jpg|thumb|[[Cessna Skymaster]] 2015 [[Israel]]]]
[[File:Alpha jet H - Cloud seeding - Royal Moroccan Air Force.jpg|thumb|[[Alpha jet]] 2014 [[Morocco]]]]

In 1891, [[Louis Gathmann]] suggested shooting [[liquid carbon dioxide]] into rain clouds to cause them to rain.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Hodges |first=Benjamin |date=2023 |title=Future Clouds: Diatoms, Speculation and Weather Modification |url=https://revistes.ub.edu/index.php/coolabah/article/view/43190 |journal=Coolabah |issue=34 |pages=112–120|doi=10.1344/co202334112-120 |s2cid=259964696 |doi-access=free }}</ref> During the 1930s, the [[Bergeron–Findeisen process]] theorized that supercooled water droplets present, while ice crystals are released into rain clouds, would cause rain. While researching [[aircraft icing]], [[General Electric]] (GE)'s [[Vincent Schaefer]] and [[Irving Langmuir]] confirmed the theory.<ref name="ley196102">{{Cite magazine
 |last=Ley
 |first=Willy
 |date=February 1961
 |title=Let's Do Something About the Weather
 |department=For Your Information
 |url=https://archive.org/stream/Galaxy_v19n03_1961-02#page/n36/mode/1up 
 |magazine=Galaxy Science Fiction
 |pages=72–84
}}</ref> Schaefer discovered the principle of cloud seeding in July 1946 through a series of serendipitous events. Following ideas  he and Langmuir generated while climbing Mt. Washington in New Hampshire, Schaefer, Langmuir's research associate, created a way of experimenting with supercooled clouds using a deep freeze unit of potential agents to stimulate ice crystal growth, i.e., table salt, talcum powder, soils, dust, and various chemical agents with minor effect. Then, on July 14, 1946, he wanted to try a few experiments at GE's [[Schenectady, New York|Schenectady]] Research Lab.{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

He was dismayed to find that the deep freezer was not cold enough to produce a "cloud" using breath air. He decided to move the process along by adding a chunk of [[dry ice]] just to lower the temperature of his experimental chamber. To his astonishment, as soon as he breathed into the deep freezer, he noted a bluish haze, followed by an eye-popping display of millions of microscopic ice crystals, reflecting the strong light rays from the lamp illuminating a cross-section of the chamber. He instantly realized that he had discovered a way to change super-cooled water into ice crystals. The experiment was easily replicated, and he explored the temperature gradient to establish the {{convert|-40|C|F}} limit for liquid water.<ref>{{Cite web | url=http://www.nmt.edu/about/history/storms/chap3.htm | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080306070626/http://www.nmt.edu/about/history/storms/chap3.htm | url-status=dead | archive-date=2008-03-06 | title=Storms Above the Desert – Chap. 3: Tampering with the Weather}}</ref>

Within the month, Schaefer's colleague, the atmospheric scientist [[Bernard Vonnegut]], was credited with discovering another method for "seeding" super-cooled cloud water. Vonnegut accomplished his discovery at the desk, looking up information in a basic chemistry text and then tinkering with silver and iodide chemicals to produce silver iodide. Together with Professor Henry Chessin, of [[University at Albany, SUNY|SUNY Albany]], a crystallographer, he co-authored a publication in ''Science''<ref>{{Cite journal| doi = 10.1126/science.174.4012.945| pmid = 17773193| year = 1971| last1 = Vonnegut | first1 = B.| last2 = Chessin | first2 = H.| title = Ice Nucleation by Coprecipitated Silver Iodide and Silver Bromide.| volume = 174| issue = 4012| pages = 945–946| journal = Science |bibcode = 1971Sci...174..945V | s2cid = 37459080}}</ref> and received a patent in 1975.<ref>"Freezing Nucleant", Bernard Vonnegut, Henry Chessin, and Richard E. Passarelli, Jr., #3,877,642, April 15, 1975</ref> Both methods were adopted for use in cloud seeding during 1946 while working for GE in the state of New York.

Schaefer's method altered a cloud's heat budget; Vonnegut's altered formative crystal structure, an ingenious property related to a good match in [[lattice constant]] between the two types of [[crystal]]. (The [[crystallography]] of ice later played a role in Vonnegut's brother [[Kurt Vonnegut]]'s novel ''[[Cat's Cradle]]''). The first attempt to modify natural clouds in the field through "cloud seeding" began during a flight that began in upstate [[New York (state)|New York]] on 13 November 1946. Schaefer was able to cause snow to fall near [[Mount Greylock]] in western Massachusetts after he dumped {{convert|6|lb|kg|round=0.5|spell=in}} of dry ice into the target cloud from a plane after a {{convert|60|mi|km|round=10|adj=on}} easterly chase from the [[Schenectady County Airport]].<ref>Ted Steinberg, ''Acts of God: The Unnatural History of Natural Disaster in America'', (Oxford University Press, 2000), p. 128.</ref>

Dry ice and silver iodide agents are effective in changing super-cooled clouds' physical chemistry, and thus useful in augmenting winter snowfall over mountains and, under certain conditions, in lightning and hail suppression. While not a new technique, [[hygroscopic]] seeding for enhancement of rainfall in warm clouds is enjoying a revival, based on positive indications from research in South Africa, Mexico, and elsewhere. 
{{citation needed|date=March 2025}} The hygroscopic material most commonly used is table salt. It is postulated that hygroscopic seeding causes the droplet size spectrum in clouds to become more maritime (bigger drops) and less continental, stimulating rainfall through coalescence.{{citation needed|date=March 2025}} From 1967 to 1972, the U.S. military's [[Operation Popeye]] cloud-seeded silver iodide to extend the monsoon season over [[North Vietnam]], specifically the [[Ho Chi Minh Trail]]. The operation extended the monsoon period an average of 30 to 45 days in the targeted areas.<ref>{{Cite web | url=http://www.acig.org/artman/publish/article_348.shtml | title=Welcome to the Air Combat Information Group | access-date=2007-02-16 | archive-date=2012-03-31 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120331234043/http://www.acig.org/artman/publish/article_348.shtml | url-status=usurped }}</ref> The 54th Weather Reconnaissance Squadron carried out the operation to "make mud, not war".<ref>[http://www.willthomas.net/Chemtrails/Articles/Weather_Warfare.htm www.willthomas.net/Chemtrails/Articles/Weather_Warfare] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070223114213/http://www.willthomas.net/Chemtrails/Articles/Weather_Warfare.htm |date=February 23, 2007 }}</ref>

An attempt by the U.S. military to modify hurricanes in the [[Atlantic basin]] using cloud seeding in the 1960s was called [[Project STORMFURY]]. Scientists tested four hurricanes across eight days and observed decreased wind speeds of 10% to 30% on four of these days. They originally attributed the lack of results to faulty execution, but the results came into question because of the lack of supercooled water in the hurricane and the inability to determine if the effects were due to human intervention or the natural processes of hurricanes.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Willoughby |first=H. E. |title=
Project STORMFURY: A Scientific Chronicle 1962–1983
 |journal=
Bulletin of the American Meteorological Society
 |pages=505–514 |date=1 May 1985 |volume=66 |issue=5 |doi=10.1175/1520-0477(1985)066<0505:PSASC>2.0.CO;2 |bibcode=1985BAMS...66..505W |doi-access=free }}</ref>

Two federal agencies have supported various weather modification research projects, which began in the early 1960s: The [[United States Bureau of Reclamation]] (Reclamation; Department of the Interior) and the [[National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration]] (NOAA; Department of Commerce). Reclamation sponsored several cloud-seeding research projects under the umbrella of Project Skywater from 1964 to 1988, and NOAA conducted the Atmospheric Modification Program from 1979 to 1993. The sponsored projects were carried out in several states and two countries (Thailand and Morocco), studying both winter and summer cloud seeding. From 1962 to 1988 Reclamation developed cloud seeding applied research to augment water supplies in the western U.S. The research focused on winter orographic seeding to enhance snowfall in the Rocky Mountains and Sierra Nevada, and precipitation in coast ranges of southern California. In California Reclamation partnered with the California Department of Water Resources ([http://www.water.ca.gov/ CDWR]) to sponsor the Serra Cooperative Pilot Project (SCPP), based in [[Auburn, California|Auburn]], to conduct seeding experiments in the central Sierra. The University of Nevada and [[Desert Research Institute]] provided cloud physics, physical chemistry, and other field support. The High Plains Cooperative Pilot Project (HIPLEX) focused on convective cloud seeding to increase rainfall during the growing season in Montana, Kansas, and Texas from 1974 to 1979.

In 1979, the [[World Meteorological Organization]] and other member-states led by the [[Government of Spain]] conducted a Precipitation Enhancement Project (PEP) in Spain,<ref>[http://www3.austlii.edu.au/au/other/dfat/treaties/1979/14.html "Agreement on the Precipitation Enhancement Project (PEP) between the World Meteorological Organization, the Government of Spain and other Member States of the World Meteorological Organization participating in the Experiment ATS 14 of 1979 "] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170415201927/http://www3.austlii.edu.au/au/other/dfat/treaties/1979/14.html |date=2017-04-15 }}. Australasian Legal Information Institute, Australian Treaties Library. Retrieved on 15 April 2017.</ref> with inconclusive results due probably to location selection issues.<ref>{{cite journal |last=List |first=Roland |title=The Precipitation Enhancement Project of the World Meteorological Organization, Program and Progress |url=https://journalofweathermodification.org/index.php/JWM/article/view/60 |journal=The Journal of Weather Modification |access-date=30 April 2020 |pages=203–209 |date=1 January 1981 |volume=13 |issue=1 |archive-date=28 July 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200728163640/https://journalofweathermodification.org/index.php/JWM/article/view/60 |url-status=live }}</ref>

Reclamation sponsored research at several universities, including Colorado State University, the Universities of Wyoming, Washington, UCLA, Utah, Chicago, NYU, Montana, and Colorado, and research teams at Stanford, Meteorology Research Inc., and Penn State University, and the South Dakota School of Mines and Technology, North Dakota, Texas A&M, Texas Tech, and Oklahoma. Cooperative efforts with state water resources agencies in California, Colorado, Montana, Kansas, Oklahoma, Texas, and Arizona assured that the applied research met state water management needs. HIPLEX also partnered with NASA, Environment Canada, and the National Center for Atmospheric Research ([http://ncar.ucar.edu/ NCAR]). From 2002 to 2006, in cooperation with six western states, Reclamation sponsored a small cooperative research program called the Weather Damage Modification Program.<ref>[http://www.naiwmc.org/NAIWMC/wdmp.html Home --> WDMP] ''www.naiwmc.org'' {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060811053854/http://www.naiwmc.org/NAIWMC/wdmp.html |date=August 11, 2006 }}</ref>

In the U.S., funding for research has declined in the last two decades. But the Bureau of Reclamation sponsored a six-state research program from 2002 to 2006 called the "Weather Damage Modification Program".<ref>Hunter, Steven M. (12 January 2005) [http://ams.confex.com/ams/Annual2005/techprogram/paper_82476.htm "The Weather Damage Modification Program"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20050107082844/http://ams.confex.com/ams/Annual2005/techprogram/paper_82476.htm |date=2005-01-07 }}. Retrieved 27 November 2009</ref> A 2003 study by the [[United States National Academy of Sciences]] urges a national research program to clear up remaining questions about weather modification's efficacy and practice.<ref>[https://www.nap.edu/catalog/10829/critical-issues-in-weather-modification-research Critical Issues in Weather Modification Research]''newton.nap.edu'' {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161023210050/https://www.nap.edu/catalog/10829/critical-issues-in-weather-modification-research |date=October 23, 2016 }}</ref>

In [[Australia]], the [[Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation]] (CSIRO) conducted major trials between 1947 and the early-1960s:

* 1947–1952: CSIRO scientists dropped dry ice into the tops of cumulus clouds. The method worked reliably with clouds that were very cold, producing rain that would not have otherwise fallen.
* 1953–1956: CSIRO carried out similar trials in [[South Australia]], [[Queensland]] and other states. Experiments used both ground-based and airborne [[silver iodide]] generators.
* Late 1950s and early 1960s: Cloud seeding in the [[Snowy Mountains]], on the [[Cape York Peninsula]] in Queensland, in the [[New England (Australia)|New England]] District of [[New South Wales]], and in the [[Warragamba]] catchment area west of [[Sydney]].

Only the trial conducted in the Snowy Mountains produced statistically significant rainfall increases over the entire experiment.<ref>{{Cite web |title=A Critical Review of the Australian Experience in Cloud Seeding |url=https://journals.ametsoc.org/view/journals/bams/78/2/1520-0477_1997_078_0239_acrota_2_0_co_2.pdf |access-date=2025-07-14 |website=journals.ametsoc.org}}</ref>{{Citation needed|date=October 2016}}

[[Hydro Tasmania]] (at the time still known as the Hydro Electric Commission) began experimenting with cloud-seeding over lake catchments in central [[Tasmania]] in the early 1960s in order to determine if their electricity-producing dams could be kept at high water levels through cloud seeding. Tasmania proved to be one place where cloud seeding was highly effective. Various trials were undertaken between 1964 and 2005, and again between 2009 and 2016, but none have taken place since then. Hydro Tasmania also undertook soil and water survey samples and found negligible trace elements of the materials used for cloud seeding (such as silver iodine), and determined it did not have a detrimental effect on the environment.

An Austrian study<ref>[http://www.hagelabwehr.com/links/studie.pdf Hagelabwehr in Niederösterreich] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090327034151/http://www.hagelabwehr.com/links/studie.pdf |date=March 27, 2009 }}, Zentralanstalt für Meteorologie und Geodynamik</ref> to use silver iodide seeding for hail prevention ran during 1981–2000, and the technique is still actively deployed there.<ref>{{cite web |title=Die Hagelflieger stellen sich vor |trans-title=The hail pilots introduce themselves |url=http://www.hagelabwehr.com/index.php?id=20&no_cache=1 |website=www.hagelabwehr.com |access-date=2016-10-23 |archive-date=2016-10-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161023202419/http://www.hagelabwehr.com/index.php?id=20&no_cache=1 |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Asia ===
==== China ====
[[File:20241013 Xi'an MA60 of Sichuan Tri-Star General Aviation (B-3435) taking off from CGO 03.jpg|thumb|[[Xian MA60]] cloud seeding aircraft of [[China Meteorological Administration]]]]
The largest cloud seeding system is in the [[People's Republic of China]]. They believe that it increases the amount of rain over several increasingly [[arid]] regions, including its capital city, [[Beijing]], by firing silver iodide [[rocket]]s into the sky where rain is desired. There is even political strife caused by neighboring regions that accuse each other of "stealing rain" using cloud seeding.<ref>{{Cite magazine
| title = Melinda Liu: Can China Control Olympics Weather?
| first = Melinda
| last = Liu
| magazine = Newsweek
| date = June 1, 2006
| access-date = 29 February 2020
| url = https://www.newsweek.com/melinda-liu-can-china-control-olympics-weather-110875
| quote = And the rainmaking scramble became so intense in 2004 that five Henan province villages reportedly squabbled over "cloud theft" after they all seeded the clouds simultaneously but only one district received the lion's share of rain.
| archive-date = 29 February 2020
| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20200229233617/https://www.newsweek.com/melinda-liu-can-china-control-olympics-weather-110875
| url-status = live
}}</ref>  China used cloud seeding in [[Beijing]] just before the 2008 Olympic Games in order to have a dry Olympic season.<ref>[http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/news/world/2006-06-29-china-rain_x.htm China rolls out the big guns, aiming for a dry Olympics] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170124045738/http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/news/world/2006-06-29-china-rain_x.htm |date=2017-01-24 }}. ''USA Today'' 6/29/2006 9:04 pm ET.</ref> In February 2009, China also blasted iodide sticks over Beijing to artificially induce snowfall after four months of drought, and blasted iodide sticks over other areas of northern China to increase snowfall. The snowfall in Beijing lasted for approximately three days and led to the closure of 12 main roads around Beijing.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/world/asia-pacific/7899086.stm China Lets it Snow to End Drought] . ''BBC'' 19 February 2009.</ref>  At the end of October 2009 Beijing claimed it had its earliest snowfall since 1987 due to cloud seeding.<ref>Branigan, Tania. (2 November 2009) "[https://www.theguardian.com/world/2009/nov/02/china-snow-beijing Nature gets a helping hand as snow blankets Beijing] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170118092243/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2009/nov/02/china-snow-beijing |date=2017-01-18 }}" ''Guardian''. Retrieved 26 November 2009</ref> According to "research paper from Tsinghua University, the Chinese weather authorities used weather modification to ensure the sky was clear and lower air pollution" on July 1, 2021. The Chinese Communist party celebrated its centenary on July 1 with a major celebration. The celebration took place in Tiananmen Square. The paper was published on November 26, 2021 in a peer-review journal called Environment Science (via South China Morning Post).{{Citation needed|date=October 2024|reason=Name of paper, authors and link were not mentioned. Better cite it because we can't tell if the paper really exists.}} The research shows that the Chinese government used cloud-seeding techniques to force rainfall the evening before the celebration event. This rainfall lowered the amount of PM2.5 pollution by more than two-thirds.{{citation needed|date=July 2025}} That helped improve the air quality at the time from "moderate" to "good".

==== India ====
In [[India]], cloud seeding operations were conducted during the years 1983, 1984–87, and 1993–94 by the Tamil Nadu Government due to severe drought.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.ceawmt.in/indian_history.html |title=CASWMT |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111123025420/http://www.ceawmt.in/indian_history.html |archive-date=November 23, 2011 }}</ref> In the years 1999 to 2004, 2006 and 2010 Karnataka government attempted cloud seeding without much success.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2012-06-15 |title=Cloud seeding is a failed mission in Karnataka |url=https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/city/bengaluru/cloud-seeding-is-a-failed-mission-in-karnataka/articleshow/14151806.cms |access-date=2025-10-29 |work=The Times of India |issn=0971-8257}}</ref> Cloud seeding operations were also conducted in 2003 and 2004 through in the state of [[Maharashtra]] with inconclusive results.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://dst.gov.in/admin_finance/un-sq1207.htm |title=Cloud Seeding |last=Sibal |first=Shri Kapil |date=2005-08-04 |publisher=Department of Science and Technology |access-date=26 November 2009 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090711163541/http://dst.gov.in/admin_finance/un-sq1207.htm |archive-date=July 11, 2009 }}</ref>

In October 2025, cloud seeding was conducted to improve the [[Air pollution in Delhi|air quality over Delhi]] but the experiment yielded no significant results.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2025-10-29 |title=No artificial rain after cloud seeding: Experts decode why Delhi's Rs 3.2 crore experiment fell flat |url=https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/city/delhi/artificial-rain-costly-and-unsustainable-measure-say-experts/articleshow/124880965.cms |access-date=2025-10-29 |work=The Times of India |issn=0971-8257}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-10-29 |title=Silver flares fired into clouds: Delhi takes two shots at summoning rain |url=https://indianexpress.com/article/cities/delhi/delhis-cloud-seeding-op-kicks-off-aircraft-takes-off-from-kanpur-where-will-it-rain-10331824/ |access-date=2025-10-29 |website=The Indian Express |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Srivastava |first=Vanita |date=2025-10-24 |title=Delhi turns to cloud seeding as air quality crisis deepens |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/d44151-025-00191-2 |journal=Nature India |language=en |doi=10.1038/d44151-025-00191-2 |issn=1755-3180|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==== Iran ====
In 1946, the Iranian government tried to fertilize Iran's clouds with the help of Americans, but it was unsuccessful.  Then in 1947, in Article 19 of the Law on Water and its Nationalization, the then Ministry of Water and Electricity was obliged to provide the water needed by the country in various ways, including cloud fertilization. Accordingly, the Ministry of Energy between 1953 and 1957, in cooperation with a Canadian company and using aircraft and silver iodide compound fertilized the clouds that were over the Karaj and Jajrud dam area.<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |date=2024-01-13 |title=سابقه بارورسازی ابرها در ایران/ بین سال‌های ١٣۵٣ تا ١٣۵٧ ابرها در یک منطقه بارور می‌شدند |url=https://www.khabaronline.ir/news/1860119/%D8%B3%D8%A7%D8%A8%D9%82%D9%87-%D8%A8%D8%A7%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%B3%D8%A7%D8%B2%DB%8C-%D8%A7%D8%A8%D8%B1%D9%87%D8%A7-%D8%AF%D8%B1-%D8%A7%DB%8C%D8%B1%D8%A7%D9%86-%D8%A8%DB%8C%D9%86-%D8%B3%D8%A7%D9%84-%D9%87%D8%A7%DB%8C-%D9%A1%D9%A3%DB%B5%D9%A3-%D8%AA%D8%A7-%D9%A1%D9%A3%DB%B5%D9%A7-%D8%A7%D8%A8%D8%B1%D9%87%D8%A7 |access-date=2024-04-19 |website=www.khabaronline.ir |language=fa}}</ref><ref name=":5">{{Cite web |date=2024-04-19 |title=بارورسازی ابرها روش جدید مقابله با خشکسالی |url=https://donya-e-eqtesad.com/%D8%A8%D8%AE%D8%B4-%D8%B3%D8%A7%DB%8C%D8%AA-%D8%AE%D9%88%D8%A7%D9%86-62/4037268-%D8%A8%D8%A7%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%B3%D8%A7%D8%B2%DB%8C-%D8%A7%D8%A8%D8%B1%D9%87%D8%A7-%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%B4-%D8%AC%D8%AF%DB%8C%D8%AF-%D9%85%D9%82%D8%A7%D8%A8%D9%84%D9%87-%D8%A8%D8%A7-%D8%AE%D8%B4%DA%A9%D8%B3%D8%A7%D9%84%DB%8C |access-date=2024-04-19 |website=روزنامه دنیای اقتصاد |language=fa}}</ref>

After the [[Iranian Revolution|revolution]] of 1978, in the years 1989 to 1995, cloud fertilization was carried out in a scattered manner using ground generators in the heights of Shirkuh, Yazd. Then, with the announcement of the Minister of Energy in February 1996, the National Center for Cloud Fertility Research and Studies was established in Yazd and officially started working in 1997.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5" />

==== Israel ====
Israel conducted experimental cloud seeding for seven years from 2014 to 2021.<ref name="Israel">{{Cite web |url=https://www.calcalist.co.il/local/articles/0,7340,L-3890093,00.html |title=Stopping rain Enhancement project, in Hebrew |work=כלכליסט - www.calcalist.co.il |date=28 January 2021 |access-date=2021-03-25 |archive-date=2021-04-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210414233211/https://www.calcalist.co.il/local/articles/0,7340,L-3890093,00.html |url-status=live }}</ref> The practice involved emitting silver iodide from airplanes and ground stations and took place only in the northern parts of the country.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Cloud microphysical background for the Israel-4 cloud seeding experiment. |journal=Atmospheric Research|volume=158–159|pages=122–138|doi=10.1016/j.atmosres.2015.02.007|year=2015|last1=Freud|first1=Eyal|last2=Koussevitzky|first2=Hagai|last3=Goren|first3=Tom|last4=Rosenfeld|first4=Daniel|bibcode=2015AtmRe.158..122F}}</ref> Israel stopped the rain enhancement project in 2021 due to the experiment data showing that the practice was largely ineffective and expensive, and because there had been some recent years of unrelated significant rainfall.<ref name="Israel"/>

==== Kuwait ====
To counter drought and a growing population in a desert region, [[Kuwait]] is embarking on its own cloud seeding program, with the local Environment Public Authority conducting a study to gauge its viability locally.<ref>[http://news.kuwaittimes.net/website/cloud-seeding-kuwait-soon/ cloud-seeding-kuwait-soon Translated from Arabic and reported originally by Al-Qabas Newspaper, Kuwait.] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160414063756/http://news.kuwaittimes.net/website/cloud-seeding-kuwait-soon/ |date=2016-04-14 }}" ''[[Kuwait Times]]''. Retrieved 16 April 2016</ref>

==== North Korea ====
Cloud seeding projects have been carried out in North Korea since the 1960s.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last1=Kim |first1=C. |last2=Jong |first2=S.-I. |last3=Ryang |first3=C.-H. |last4=Paek |first4=U.-S. |date=August 2023 |title=An Overview of Cloud Seeding Experiments for Precipitation Enhancement over the Northern Part of the Korean Peninsula and Their Future Prospects |url=https://link.springer.com/10.3103/S1068373923080083 |journal=Russian Meteorology and Hydrology |language=en |volume=48 |issue=8 |pages=704–712 |doi=10.3103/S1068373923080083 |bibcode=2023RuMH...48..704K |issn=1068-3739|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Their initial purpose was to ensure a steady supply of water for the hydroelectric power stations located in the South Hamgyong Province. In the following decades, however, while research on utilising cloud resources was carried out, seeding operations themselves were almost nonexistent. Cloud seeding operations were prioritised again in the twenty-first century due to socio-economic requirements, and to mitigate natural disasters such as droughts and hailstorms. More recently, between 2017 and 2020, the Chungsan region was selected to carry out cloud seeding experiments over, which were seen to have enhanced precipitation in the region.<ref name=":6" />

==== Pakistan ====
Pakistan has undergone its first-ever artificial rain experiment using cloud seeding, in a move carried out with the help of the United Arab Emirates. There was drizzle in 'at least 10 areas' of Lahore, consistently ranked the most polluted city in the world.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Pakistan uses artificial rain for the first time to fight pollution |url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2023/12/16/pakistan-uses-artificial-rain-for-the-first-time-to-fight-pollution |access-date=2024-12-11 |website=Al Jazeera |language=en}}</ref>

On Friday, November 15, 2024, Pakistan successfully conducted a cloud seeding operation using locally developed technology, resulting in artificial rainfall to tackle the region’s smog crisis. The Meteorological Department confirmed rainfall in [[Jhelum]], [[Gujar Khan]], [[Chakwal]], and [[Talagang]], attributing it to the cloud seeding effort. Cloud seeding was conducted around 14:00, with subsequent rainfall observed within hours in Jhelum and Gujar Khan.<ref>{{Cite web |last=News Desk |first=tribune.com.pk |date=2024-11-15 |title=Punjab conducts cloud seeding to combat smog with locally developed technology |url=https://tribune.com.pk/story/2509839/punjab-conducts-cloud-seeding-to-combat-smog-with-locally-developed-technology |access-date=2024-12-12 |website=The Express Tribune |language=en}}</ref>

==== Sri Lanka ====
In January 2019, the [[Sri Lanka Air Force]] (SLAF) and [[Ministry of Power and Energy|Ministry of Power, Energy and Business Development]] signed an agreement for a cloud seeding project, created in response to lower water levels for [[hydroelectric power]] due to dry weather.<ref>{{cite press release |author=<!--Not stated--> |title=SLAF Embarks on Cloud Seeding Project with Ministry of Power, Energy and Business Development |url=https://www.airforce.lk/news.php?news=3956 |publisher=Sri Lanka Air Force |date=23 January 2019}}</ref> In February of that year, a group of officials from the SLAF, [[Ceylon Electricity Board]], and meteorology and irrigation departments were sent to Thailand to study rainmaking projects carried out by the [[Department of Royal Rainmaking and Agricultural Aviation]].<ref name="mongabay">{{cite news |last1=Handunnetti |first1=Dilrukshi |title=Cloud seeding bears no rain in Sri Lanka |url=https://news.mongabay.com/2019/04/when-cloud-seeding-bears-no-rain/ |publisher=Mongabay |date=12 April 2019}}</ref> On March 22, a [[Harbin Y-12]] flew over the [[Maskeliya Dam|Maskeliya Reservoir]] at {{cvt|2400|m}}, dispersing cloud seeding chemicals.<ref name="newsfirst">{{cite news |title=Cloud seeding project brings rain |date=22 March 2019 |location=Colombo |publisher=News First}}</ref> Rain arrived the next day on March 23, though project members had expected it to appear earlier on the 22nd.<ref name="mongabay"/> [[News First]] proclaimed that the pilot project had "proven to be a success",<ref name="newsfirst"/> while [[Mongabay]] described it as a "failed attempt" that had "fallen short" and highlighted various climate experts who recommended that the government conduct more research into the project's potential environmental effects before proceeding further.<ref name="mongabay"/>

==== United Arab Emirates ====
[[File:OH-BAX, Beechcraft C90 King Air (17802311604).jpg|thumb|Beechcraft [[Beechcraft King Air|King Air C90]] used for cloud seeding operations in [[UAE]]]]
{{Excerpt|Cloud seeding in the United Arab Emirates}}

=== Southeast Asia ===
In [[Southeast Asia]], open-burning [[haze]] pollutes the regional environment. Cloud seeding has been used to improve the air quality by encouraging rainfall. On 20 June 2013, [[Indonesia]] said it will begin cloud-seeding operations following reports from [[Singapore]] and [[Malaysia]] that smog caused by forest and bush fires in [[Sumatra]] have disrupted daily activities in the neighboring countries.<ref>[https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-22998592 Singapore haze hits record highs from Indonesia fires] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181203181134/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-22998592 |date=2018-12-03 }} ''www.bbc.co.uk'' 21 June 2013</ref> On 25 June 2013, hailstones were reported to have fallen over some parts of Singapore.<ref>[http://www.straitstimes.com/breaking-news/singapore/story/hail-reported-the-western-end-singapore-20130625 "Hail" reported in the Western end of Singapore] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130924231808/http://www.straitstimes.com/breaking-news/singapore/story/hail-reported-the-western-end-singapore-20130625 |date=2013-09-24 }} ''www.straitstimes.com''</ref> Despite [[National Environment Agency|NEA]] denials, some believe that the hailstones are the result of cloud seeding in Indonesia.

==== Indonesia ====
In [[Jakarta]], cloud seeding was tested in 2013 to minimize flood risk in anticipation of heavy floods, according to the Agency for the Assessment and Application of Technology.<ref>[http://jakartaglobe.beritasatu.com/archive/bppt-to-use-cloud-seeding-to-minimize-flood-risk-in-jakarta/ bppt-to-use-cloud-seeding-to-minimize-flood-risk-in-jakarta] ''jakartaglobe.beritasatu.com'' {{Dead link|date=January 2019 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> The practice has continued up to March 2025, national and provincial disaster agencies used cloud seeding over the sea north of Jakarta, with the aim of shifting rainfall away from land and toward the sea, costing about 200 million rupiahs (US$12,292) for each flyover.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-07-06 |title=Floods Inundate Jakarta After Cuts to Disaster Relief |url=https://globalpressjournal.com/asia/indonesia/floods-inundate-jakarta-cuts-disaster-relief/ |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=Global Press Journal |language=en-US}}</ref>

==== Malaysia ====
In Malaysia, cloud seeding was first used in 1988 for three purposes: filling up dams, lessening the effects of haze, and fighting forest fires.<ref>{{cite web|title=Malaysia is using Cloud Seeding to Beat the Haze ... But How Much does it Cost?|url=https://cilisos.my/malaysias-dams-are-drying-up-fast-but-making-rain-to-refill-them-wont-be-cheap/|website=Cili Sos|access-date=3 May 2023|date=17 September 2019|author=Badd}}</ref> In 2015, cloud seeding was done daily in [[Malaysia]] since the [[2015 Southeast Asian haze|haze]] began in early-August.<ref>{{cite web|title=Cloud seeding done daily|url=http://news.asiaone.com/news/malaysia/cloud-seeding-done-daily|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151007235021/http://news.asiaone.com/news/malaysia/cloud-seeding-done-daily|url-status=dead|archive-date=October 7, 2015|website=news.asiaone.com|access-date=7 October 2015}}</ref> Johor Water Regulatory Body focused to produce rain over dams with critically low water levels. They used [[Cessna 340]] with tubes of ioidised salt, flying from their operation base at [[Senai International Airport]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nst.com.my/news/2015/10/cloud-seeding-aid-sungai-layang-and-sungai-lebam-dams-commences |title=Cloud seeding to aid Sungai Layang and Sungai Lebam dams commences |last=Said |first=Halim|date=2015-10-12 |publisher=New Straits Times|access-date=2 March 2024}}</ref>

==== Thailand ====
{{main|Royal Rainmaking Project}}

[[File:KASET-1512 (14107332963).jpg|thumb|[[CASA C-212 Aviocar]] of KASET [[Thailand]]]]
The Thailand [[Royal Rainmaking Project]] ({{langx|th|โครงการฝนหลวง}}, {{RTGS|''khrongkan fon luang''}}) was initiated in November 1955 by [[King Bhumibol Adulyadej]]. Thai farmers repeatedly suffered the effects of drought. The king resolved to do something about it and proposed a solution to the dearth of rain: artificial [[rainmaking]], or cloud seeding. The program is run by the Department of Royal Rainmaking and Agricultural Aviation. Thailand started a rain-making project in the late-1950s, known today as the [[Royal Rainmaking Project]]. Its first efforts scattered sea salt in the air to catch the humidity and dry ice to condense the humidity to form clouds.<ref>[https://archive.today/20130207180418/http://164.115.100.119/RRMThaiGov/httpdocs/story1-2.php "The Rainmaking Story"], ''Bureau of Royal Rainmaking and Agricultural Aviation''{{dead link|date=July 2014}}</ref> The project took about ten years of experiments and refinement. The first field operations began in 1969 above [[Khao Yai National Park]]. Since then the Thai government claims that rainmaking has been successfully applied throughout Thailand and neighboring countries.<ref>[http://www.thaiembassy.se/minmapp/filer/pdf-pages/modernmonarchy.pdf  "Modern Monarchy"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121115093503/http://www.thaiembassy.se/minmapp/filer/pdf-pages/modernmonarchy.pdf |date=2012-11-15 }}, Royal Thai Embassy Stockholm</ref> The king received recognition for the Royal Rainmaking Project from the [[Eureka (organization)|Eureka organization]] in 2001 for an invention that is beneficial to the world. In 2009, Jordan received permission from Thailand to use the technique. On 12 October 2005 the [[European Patent Office]] granted to King [[Bhumibol Adulyadej]] the patent EP 1 491 088 ''Weather modification by royal rainmaking technology''.<ref>{{cite web|title=Weather modification by royal rainmaking technology|url=https://patents.google.com/patent/US20050056705|website=Google Patents|access-date=23 October 2016|date=17 March 2005|archive-date=19 October 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171019214452/http://www.google.com/patents/US20050056705|url-status=live}}</ref> The budget of the Department of Royal Rainmaking and Agricultural Aviation in [[Fiscal year|FY2019]] was 2,224 million [[Thai baht|baht]].<ref>{{cite book |title=Thailand's Budget in Brief Fiscal Year 2019 |date=September 2018 |publisher=Bureau of the Budget |location=Bangkok |page=85 |url=http://www.bb.go.th/en/topic-detail.php?id=8562&mid=456&catID=0 |access-date=9 October 2019 |archive-date=12 December 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191212200901/http://www.bb.go.th/en/topic-detail.php?id=8562&mid=456&catID=0 |url-status=live }}</ref>

==== Vietnam ====
In [[Vietnam]], during the leadup to and initial stages of the [[Battle of Điện Biên Phủ]] in 1954, the [[French Far East Expeditionary Corps]] looked into the possibility of using cloud seeding to impede [[Việt Minh]] flow of supplies through Route Provinciale 41, a dirt road leading into Điện Biên Phủ that would become more difficult to navigate during the [[monsoon|rainy season]]. General [[Henri Navarre]] authorized research into using cloud seeding this way on March 16, just before the outbreak of the battle, and a test commenced the following month. Results were disappointing; while it did not take long for the rain clouds to form and release precipitation, they often drifted away from Route Provinciale 41 in the process, reducing their ability to hinder Việt Minh logistics.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Rocolle |first1=Pierre |title=Pourquoi Dien Bien Phu? |date=1968 |publisher=Flammarion |location=Paris |pages=410–411 |language=French}}</ref>

===North America===
====United States====
{{See also|Project Stormfury}}
In the United States, cloud seeding is used to increase precipitation in areas experiencing drought, to reduce the size of hailstones that form in thunderstorms, and to reduce the amount of fog in and around airports. In the summer of 1948, the usually humid city of [[Alexandria, Louisiana|Alexandria]], Louisiana, under Mayor [[Carl B. Close]], seeded a cloud with dry ice at the municipal airport during a drought; quickly {{convert|0.85|in|mm}} of rain fell.<ref>''Alexandria Daily Town Talk'', June 29, 1948</ref>{{better source needed|date=February 2025}}

Major [[ski resort]]s occasionally use cloud seeding to induce snowfall. Eleven western states and one Canadian province (Alberta) had ongoing weather modification operational programs in 2012.<ref>{{Cite web | url=http://www.nawmc.org/ | title=North American Weather Modification Council | website=www.nawmc.org | access-date=2013-01-31 | archive-date=2012-11-01 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121101015203/http://www.nawmc.org/ | url-status=live }}</ref> In 2006, an $8.8 million project began in [[Wyoming]] to examine cloud seeding's effects on snowfall over Wyoming's [[Medicine Bow Mountains|Medicine Bow]], [[Sierra Madre Range (Wyoming)|Sierra Madre]], and [[Wind River Range|Wind River]] mountain ranges.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2006-01/ncfa-wcs012606.php|title=Wyoming cloud seeding experiment begins this month|last=National Center for Atmospheric Research|date=26 January 2006|work=Eureka Alert|access-date=27 November 2009|archive-date=22 November 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091122214924/http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2006-01/ncfa-wcs012606.php|url-status=live}}</ref>

In Oregon, Portland General Electric used Hood River seeding to produce snow for hydro power in 1974-1975. The results were substantial, but caused an undue burden on the locals, who experienced overpowering rainfall, causing street collapses and mudslides. PGE discontinued its seeding practices the next year.<ref>: Book; Mountain Geography; Physical and Human dimensions by M.F. Price, A.C. Byers, D.A. Friend, T. Kohler, L.W. Price</ref>

In 1978, the U.S. signed the [[Environmental Modification Convention]], which bans the use of weather modification for hostile purposes.<ref>{{Cite web | url=http://www.un-documents.net/enmod.htm | website=www.un-documents.net | title=Convention on the Prohibition of Military or Any Other Hostile Use of Environmental Modification Techniques – A/RES/31/72 Annex – UN Documents: Gathering a body of global agreements | access-date=2011-05-13 | archive-date=2022-04-21 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220421115817/http://www.un-documents.net/enmod.htm | url-status=live }}</ref>

As of 2022, seven agencies in California are conducting cloud seeding operations using silver iodide, including the [[Sacramento Municipal Utility District]], which began employing the technique in 1969 to increase the water supply to its hydroelectric power plants, and reported that it results in "an average of 3 to 10% increase in [<nowiki/>[[Sierra Nevada]]] snowpack".<ref>{{Cite web |last=Cabanatuan |first=Michael |date=2022-03-16 |title=A plane was 'cloud seeding' in the Sierra this week to make it rain. Does that actually work? |url=https://www.sfchronicle.com/california/article/An-aircraft-was-cloud-seeding-in-the-Sierra-17007402.php |access-date=2022-09-18 |website=San Francisco Chronicle |language=en-US}}</ref>

====Canada====
During the sixties, Irving P. Krick & Associates operated a successful cloud seeding operation in the area around [[Calgary]], [[Alberta]]. This utilized both aircraft and ground-based generators that pumped silver iodide into the atmosphere in an attempt to reduce the threat of hail damage. Ralph Langeman, [[Lynn Garrison]], and Stan McLeod, all ex-members of the RCAF's 403 Squadron, attending the University of Alberta, spent their summers flying hail suppression. The Alberta Hail Suppression Project is continuing with C$3 million a year in funding from insurance companies to reduce hail damage in southern Alberta.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/calgary/alberta-s-cloud-seeding-pilots-see-2nd-busiest-year-in-20-years-1.2744786|title=Alberta's cloud-seeding pilots see 2nd busiest year in 20 years|publisher=[[CBC News]]|date=22 August 2014|access-date=23 August 2014|archive-date=26 August 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140826103809/http://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/calgary/alberta-s-cloud-seeding-pilots-see-2nd-busiest-year-in-20-years-1.2744786|url-status=live}}</ref>

===Europe===
[[File:Hagelflieger-EDTD.jpg|thumb|[[Partenavia P.68]] Hagelflieger - [[Germany]]]]

==== Bulgaria ====
Bulgaria operates a national network of hail protection, silver iodide rocket sites, strategically located in agricultural areas such as the [[Rose Valley, Bulgaria|rose valley]]. Each site protects an area of 10 sq. km (4 sq. mi.), the density of the site clusters is such that at least 2 sites will be able to target a single hail cloud, initial detection of hail cloud formation to firing of the rockets is typically 7–10 minutes in its entire process with a view to seed the formation of much smaller hailstones, high in the atmosphere that will melt before reaching ground level.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://bnr.bg/en/post/100577459/masters-of-hailstorms|title=Masters of hailstorms|access-date=2018-04-24|website=bnr.bg|archive-date=2018-04-24|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180424202943/http://bnr.bg/en/post/100577459/masters-of-hailstorms|url-status=live}}</ref>

Data collated since the 1960s suggests huge agricultural sector losses are avoided yearly with the protection system, unseeded the hail will flatten entire regions, with seeding this can be reduced to minor leaf damage from the smaller hailstones that failed to melt.{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

====France and Spain====
Cloud seeding began in France during the 1950s with the intent of reducing hail damage to crops. The [[ANELFA]]  ([http://www.anelfa.asso.fr]) project consists of local agencies acting within a non-profit organization.<ref name=francespain>{{cite web|url=http://www.essl.org/ECSS/2009/preprints/P12-02-dessens.pdf|publisher=5th European Conference on Severe Storms|title=Mitigation of Hail Damages by Cloud Seeding in France and Spain|access-date=2010-11-21|archive-date=2010-12-09|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101209120420/http://essl.org/ECSS/2009/preprints/P12-02-dessens.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref> A similar project in Spain is managed by the Consorcio por la Lucha Antigranizo de Aragon.<ref name=francespain/> The success of the French program was supported by analysis made by Jean Dessens based on insurance data; that of the Spanish program in studies conducted by the Spanish Agricultural Ministry.<ref name=francespain/> However, Jean Dessens's results were heavily criticized and doubt was cast on the effectiveness of ground generator seeding. ([https://journals.ametsoc.org/view/journals/apme/26/12/1520-0450_1987_026_1774_coisfp_2_0_co_2.xml?tab_body=pdf])

====Russia====
The [[Soviet Union]] created a specifically designed version of the [[Antonov An-30]] aerial survey aircraft, the An-30M Sky Cleaner, with eight containers of solid [[carbon dioxide]] in the cargo area plus external pods containing meteorological cartridges that could be fired into clouds.<ref name=gunston95>{{ref book osprey encyc of russian aircraft|1}}</ref> Russian government spends millions on 'cloud seeding' technology to ensure it doesn't rain on May Day public holiday.
