contested.title: Ferenc Gyurcsány
contested.raw_text:
{{Short description|Prime Minister of Hungary from 2004 to 2009}} {{POV|date=March 2024}} {{Use dmy dates|date=October 2021}} {{Western name order|Gyurcsány Ferenc}} {{Infobox officeholder | name = Ferenc Gyurcsány | image = Ferenc Gyurcsany 2012.jpg | caption = Gyurcsány in 2004 | office = [[Prime Minister of Hungary]] | term_start = 29 September 2004 | term_end = 14 April 2009 | president = {{plainlist| * [[Ferenc Mádl]] * [[László Sólyom]]}} | deputy = | predecessor = [[Péter Medgyessy]] | successor = [[Gordon Bajnai]] | office1 = President of the [[Democratic Coalition (Hungary)|Democratic Coalition]] | term_start1 = 22 October 2011 | term_end1 = 8 May 2025 | predecessor1 = ''Party established'' | successor1 = [[Klára Dobrev]] | office2 = President of the [[Hungarian Socialist Party]] | term_start2 = 27 February 2007 | term_end2 = 5 April 2009 | predecessor2 = [[István Hiller]] | successor2 = [[Ildikó Lendvai]] | office3 = [[Government of Hungary|Minister of Youth Affairs and Sports]] | term_start3 = 19 May 2003 | term_end3 = 4 October 2004 | prime_minister3 = [[Péter Medgyessy]]
''Himself'' | deputy3 = | predecessor3 = [[György Jánosi]] | successor3 = [[Kinga Göncz]] | office4 = [[National Assembly (Hungary)|Member of the National Assembly]] | term_start4 = 16 May 2006 | term_end4 = 9 May 2025 | constituency4 = National List | birth_date = {{birth date and age|1961|06|04|df=y}} | birth_place = [[Pápa]], [[People's Republic of Hungary|Hungary]] | death_date = | death_place = | spouse = Beatrix Rozs (divorced)
{{marriage|Edina Bognár|1985||end=div}}
{{marriage|[[Klára Dobrev]]|1995|2025|end=separated}} | children = 5 | parents = | party = [[Democratic Coalition (Hungary)|DK]] (2011–2025) | other_party = {{plainlist| * [[Hungarian Young Communist League|KISZ]] (1980–1989) * [[Hungarian Socialist Party|MSZP]] (2000–2011)}} | alma_mater = [[University of Pécs]] | occupation = {{hlist|[[Entrepreneur]]|[[Politician]]}} }} {{Ferenc Gyurcsány sidebar}} '''Ferenc Gyurcsány'''{{efn|{{IPA|hu|ˈfɛrɛnt͡s ˈɟurt͡ʃaːɲ|-|Hu-Ferenc_Gyurcsány.ogg}}}} (born 4 June 1961) is a Hungarian [[entrepreneur]] and retired politician who served as [[Prime Minister of Hungary]] from 2004 to 2009. Prior to that, he held the position of [[Government of Hungary|Minister of Youth Affairs and Sports]] between 2003 and 2004. He was nominated as [[prime minister]] by the [[Hungarian Socialist Party]] (MSZP) on 25 August 2004, after [[Péter Medgyessy]] resigned due to a conflict with the Socialist Party's coalition partner. Gyurcsány was elected prime minister on 29 September 2004 in a parliamentary vote (197 yes votes, 12 no votes, with most of the opposition in [[National Assembly (Hungary)|Parliament]] not voting). He led his coalition to victory in the [[2006 Hungarian parliamentary election|2006 parliamentary election]], securing another term as prime minister. On 24 February 2007, he was elected as the leader of the MSZP, winning 89% of the vote. On 21 March 2009, Gyurcsány announced his intention to resign as prime minister.{{cite news | url = https://www.nytimes.com/2009/03/22/world/22hungary.html?hp | work=The New York Times | title=Hungary's Premier Offers to Resign | first=Nicholas | last=Kulish | date=22 March 2009}}{{cite web|url=http://edition.cnn.com/2009/WORLD/europe/03/21/hungary.pm/index.html|title=Hungarian PM offers to step down|access-date=14 February 2015}}{{cite news | url = https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/europe/7956610.stm | work=BBC News | title='Obstacle' Hungary PM to resign | date=21 March 2009}} President [[László Sólyom]] stated that instead of a short-term government ruling only until the 2010 elections, early elections should be held.{{cite web|url=http://www.pr-inside.com/hungary-s-president-favors-early-elections-r1141169.htm|title=Free Services for PR :: News :: Press Releases|access-date=14 February 2015|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120229092805/http://www.pr-inside.com/hungary-s-president-favors-early-elections-r1141169.htm|archive-date=29 February 2012}} On 28 March 2009 Gyurcsány resigned from his position as party chairman.[http://www.budapesttimes.hu/content/view/11530/219/]{{dead link|date=September 2016}} A minister under Gyurcsány, [[Gordon Bajnai]], became the nominee of MSZP for the post of prime minister in March 2009{{cite web|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/SB123840402537769209|title=Hungary's Ruling Party Picks Premier|author=Edith Balazs|author2=Charles Forelle|date=31 March 2009|work=WSJ|access-date=14 February 2015}} and he became prime minister on 14 April. In October 2011, Gyurcsány and other party members quit the MSZP to establish the [[Democratic Coalition (Hungary)|Democratic Coalition]] (DK) under his leadership. In May 2025, he announced his resignation from the leadership of the party, and also his retirement from politics.{{Cite web |last=Albert |first=Gazda |date=2025-05-08 |title=Gyurcsány Ferenc mindenről lemond és visszavonul a közélettől |url=https://444.hu/2025/05/08/gyurcsany-ferenc-mindenrol-lemond-es-visszavonul-a-kozelettol |access-date=2025-05-08 |website=444 |language=hu}}{{Cite web |date=2025-05-08 |title=Véget ér egy korszak: lemondott és visszavonul Gyurcsány Ferenc |url=https://mfor.hu/cikkek/kozelet/veget-er-egy-korszak-lemondott-es-visszavonul-gyurcsany-ferenc.html |access-date=2025-05-08 |website=mfor.hu - Menedzsment Fórum |language=hu}} ==Early years== Gyurcsány was born in [[Pápa]], Hungary, into an impoverished middle-class family as the only son of Ferenc Gyurcsány Sr. and Katalin Varga. He has an elder sister, Éva. According to contemporary police documents, Gyurcsány's father was convicted on charges of minor crimes (low value thefts and fraud) multiple times.{{cite web|url=http://www.stop.hu/articles/article.php?id=241488|title=Sztрrklikk - A blog magazin|website=Stop.hu|access-date=11 September 2016|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100913060343/http://www.stop.hu/articles/article.php?id=241488|archive-date=13 September 2010}} Gyurcsány attended Apáczai Csere János High School in Budapest for two years; he then returned to Pápa, Hungary and continued his studies there, at a local grammar school. In 1979, he was admitted to the [[University of Pécs]], where he studied as a teacher and obtained his B.Sc. in 1984, then he studied [[economics]] at the same institution, getting his degree in 1990. In 1981, he assumed function in the KISZ, the [[Organisation of Young Communists]], where he mostly handled organizing student programs at the beginning. Between 1984 and 1988, he was the vice president of the organisation's committee in [[Pécs]]. Then between 1988 and 1989, he was the president of the central KISZ committee of universities and colleges. After the political change in 1989, he became vice-president of the organisation's short-lived quasi successor, the [[Hungarian Democratic Youth Association]] (DEMISZ). From 1990 onwards, he transferred from the public to the private sector. Gyurcsány then took the position of CEO at Altus Ltd., a holding company of which he was owner, from 1992 to 2002 and thereafter as chairman of the board.{{cite web |url=http://www.hungria.org.br/CV_Gyurcsany.htm |title=CV Prime Minister of Hungary |access-date=6 April 2006 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060825071116/http://www.hungria.org.br/CV_Gyurcsany.htm |archive-date=25 August 2006 }} By 2002, he was listed as the fiftieth-richest person in Hungary.{{cite web|url=http://www.origo.hu/uzletinegyed/hirek/2002111541tol.html |title=41-tõl 60-ig |website=Origo.hu |date=15 November 2002 |access-date=11 September 2016}} ==Return to politics== Gyurcsány returned to politics in 2002 as the head strategic advisor of [[Péter Medgyessy]], the previous [[Prime Minister of Hungary|prime minister of Hungary]]. From May 2003 until September 2004 Gyurcsány was a [[Political minister|minister]] responsible for sports, youth and children. He became the president of the MSZP in [[Győr-Moson-Sopron]] [[Counties of Hungary|county]] in January 2004, serving until September 2004. In the summer of that same year it seemed that there were larger problems in his relationship with Prime Minister Péter Medgyessy, so he resigned as minister. In a week, problems in the coalition led to the resignation of Medgyessy, and MSZP voted Gyurcsány to become prime minister as he was acceptable for the coalition partner, [[Alliance of Free Democrats]] (SZDSZ). ==Prime minister== {{Further|First Gyurcsány Government|Second Gyurcsány Government}} {{Infobox administration | image = Ferenc Gyurcsány, Davos 2 (cropped).jpg | image_size = | caption = | name = Premiership of Ferenc Gyurcsány | term_start = 29 September 2004 | term_end = 14 April 2009 | president = [[Ferenc Mádl]]
[[László Sólyom]] | cabinet = [[First Gyurcsány Government]]
[[Second Gyurcsány Government]] | party = [[Hungarian Socialist Party|MSZP]] | election = [[2006 Hungarian parliamentary election|2006]] | predecessor = [[Péter Medgyessy#Prime Minister of Hungary|Péter Medgyessy]] | successor = [[Gordon Bajnai#Prime minister|Gordon Bajnai]] }} [[File:GyurcsanyBush2005.jpg|thumb|Gyurcsány with U.S. president [[George W. Bush]] in Washington, D.C., 7 October 2005]] Gyurcsány was reappointed as prime minister after the [[2006 Hungarian parliamentary election|2006 parliamentary elections]], with his coalition taking 210 of the available 386 parliamentary seats, and making him the first Hungarian prime minister to keep the office after a general election since 1990. On 24 February 2007, he also became the leader of his party (being the only candidate for the post), gaining 89% of the votes. However, soon after the election victory serious financial problems arose. His government was forced to implement austerity measures to somehow manage the budget deficit, which was much higher than expected and had grown to an almost 10% of the GDP by the end of 2006. These measures were heavily criticized by both the opposition, led by [[Fidesz – Hungarian Civic Union|Fidesz]], as being too harsh on the people, and by liberal economists, for not reducing spending enough on social benefits, including pensions. === Őszöd speech and protests=== {{Main|Őszöd speech|2006 protests in Hungary}} On 17 September 2006, an audio recording surfaced, allegedly from a closed-door meeting of the Prime Minister's party MSZP, held on 26 May 2006, shortly after MSZP won the election. On the [[Ferenc Gyurcsány's speech in Balatonőszöd in 2006 May|recording]], Gyurcsány admitted "We have obviously been lying for the last one and a half to two years." ("''Nyilvánvalóan végighazudtuk az utolsó másfél-két évet''.") and that "We've been lying at morning, evening, and night" ("''Hazudtunk reggel, éjjel, és este''."). The later-formed public consensus on the infamous "Őszöd speech" is that Gyurcsány was obviously drunk at the time and that he wasn't supposed to speak on the aforementioned closed-door meeting. Despite public outrage, the Prime Minister refused to resign, and a series of demonstrations started near the [[Hungarian Parliament]], swelling from 2,000 to about 8,000 demonstrators calling for the resignation of Gyurcsány and his government for several weeks. The Prime Minister admitted the authenticity of the recording.{{cite web|author=Ugró Miklós |url=http://www.hirtv.hu/?tPath=/belfold/&article_hid=115874 |title=HírTV - Hír TV |website=Hirtv.hu |access-date=11 September 2016}} [[File:Gyurcsany Ferenc-mszp-2.jpg|thumb|Gyurcsány at the Socialist Party's congress]] On 1 October, the governing party suffered a landslide defeat in the [[2006 Hungarian local elections|local municipal elections]].{{cite web|url=http://www.valasztas.hu/outroot/onkdina/g12.htm |title=Archived copy |access-date=4 November 2006 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070927004406/http://www.valasztas.hu/outroot/onkdina/g12.htm |archive-date=27 September 2007 }} On the eve of the elections, before the results were known, President [[László Sólyom]] gave a speech in which he said that the solution to the situation is in the hands of the majority in Parliament.{{cite web|url=http://index.hu/politika/belfold/soly1001/ |title=Index - Belföld - Sólyom: Most az Országgyűlésnek van cselekvési lehetősége |website=Index.hu |date=1 January 1999 |access-date=11 September 2016}} ===Vote of confidence=== As Prime Minister, Gyurcsány was a strong advocate of the [[South Stream|South Stream pipeline project]], which aimed to supply Russian gas directly to the [[European Union]] (EU), bypassing transit countries such as Ukraine. He signed the contract in Moscow just a week before the popular election in Hungary, which showed around 80% of the votes were against the government reforms. On 6 October 2006, Gyurcsány won a vote of confidence in Parliament, 207–165, with no coalition MP voting against him. The vote was public.[https://www.ft.com/content/853cdd38-55a0-11db-acba-0000779e2340 Gyurcsany wins vote of confidence], ''[[Financial Times]]'', 6 October 2006. ===Resignation=== On 21 March 2009, Gyurcsány announced his intention to resign as prime minister. He stated that he was a hindrance to further economic and social reforms. Gyurcsány asked his party to find a new candidate for prime minister in two weeks.[https://www.google.com/hostednews/ap/article/ALeqM5ia84yEP9jDmntQwdHNNV7NnsG9KwD972C81G0]{{dead link|date=June 2024|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} President [[László Sólyom]] stated that instead of a short-term transactional government ruling only until the 2010 elections, early elections should be held. In the search for a new prime minister, [[György Surányi]] became the frontrunner candidate for the post; however, on 26 March he pulled out of the race.{{cite news |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/rbssFinancialServicesAndRealEstateNews/idUSLQ97333420090326 |work=Reuters |title=SCENARIOS-What next as Hungary seeks new prime minister? |date=26 March 2009}} On 28 March, Gyurcsány resigned from his position as party chairman. ==Career after premiership== [[File:Ferenc.gyurcsany.2015(cropped).jpg|180px|thumb|Gyurcsány in 2015]] In the 2010 parliamentary elections he was elected into the Parliament still as a member of MSZP, but he became more and more critical of the party's politics. Since his attempts at reforming the party failed, he left MSZP and founded a new party, the [[Democratic Coalition (Hungary)|Democratic Coalition]] ''(Demokratikus Koalíció, DK)'' in October 2011.{{cite web|url=http://www.origo.hu/itthon/20111106-gyurcsanyt-valasztotta-partelnoknek-a-demokratikus-koalicio.html |title=Gyurcsαnyt vαlasztotta pαrtelnφknek a Demokratikus Koalνciσ |website=Origo.hu |access-date=11 September 2016}} As a leader of DK, he announced that his party would support Gordon Bajnai as a candidate for prime minister in 2014.{{cite web|url=http://www.stop.hu/belfold/gyurcsany-bajnai-gordon-legyen-a-demokratikus-ellenzek-miniszterelnok-jeloltje/1093546/|title=Gyurcsány: Bajnai Gordon legyen a demokratikus ellenzék miniszterelnök-jelöltje!|work=STOP|access-date=14 February 2015|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150214230845/http://www.stop.hu/belfold/gyurcsany-bajnai-gordon-legyen-a-demokratikus-ellenzek-miniszterelnok-jeloltje/1093546/|archive-date=14 February 2015}} In September 2012, the ruling [[Fidesz]] proposed a voter-registration plan, which, according to the opposition, "would have restricted the right to vote". Gyurcsány and three other members of his party participated in a week-long [[hunger strike]] against the proposal.[http://www.origo.hu/itthon/20120909-gyurcsany-ferenc-egyhetes-ehsegsztrajkba-kezd-a-kossuth-teren.html Gyurcsány Ferenc egyhetes éhségsztrájkba kezd a Kossuth téren] – Origo, 2012. 09. 09. Later, in January 2013, the [[Constitutional Court of Hungary]] struck down the new electoral law as unconstitutional; after that decision, Fidesz caucus dropped the law.[http://www.origo.hu/itthon/20130104-az-alkotmanybirosag-dontese-a-valasztasi-feliratkozasrol.html Alkotmányellenes a választási regisztráció] – Origo, 2013. január 4. Gyurcsány referred to that act as his party's success.{{cite web|url=http://www.demokratikuskoalicio.hu/index.php/2011-12-05-15-42-05/ajanlottblogok/3965-erlauer-gyurcsany-ferenc-nagy-sikere |title=www.demokratikuskoalicio.hu powered by Domain Technologie Control (DTC) |access-date=14 February 2015 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160306065423/http://www.demokratikuskoalicio.hu/index.php/2011-12-05-15-42-05/ajanlottblogok/3965-erlauer-gyurcsany-ferenc-nagy-sikere |archive-date=6 March 2016 }} On 14 January 2014, the Democratic Coalition and four other groups founded [[Unity (Hungary)|Unity]], a political alliance with the aim of defeating Fidesz at the elections in the spring. The alliance made it into the Parliament, but only as opposition. DK won only four seats, which meant that since they were below the minimum requirements of forming a parliamentary group (five seats), its MPs (including Gyurcsány) officially count as independent politicians. On 8 April 2018, the Democratic Coalition won nine seats, creating now a political group in the National Assembly. In the 2019 European elections, his party got 16% of the vote and became the largest opposition party until the 2024 European elections. On 8 May 2025, Gyurcsány announced that he would resign from the leadership of the Democratic Coalition and from his seat in parliament, and would retire from politics. ==Personal life== [[File:Ferenc Gyurcsany cc.jpg|200px|thumb|Gyurcsány at a music festival in Q&A.]] Gyurcsány has been married three times. He has two sons from his second marriage with Edina Bognár, and three children[http://www.origo.hu/itthon/20150219-megszuletett-gyurcsany-ferenc-kisfia.html Megszületett Gyurcsány Ferenc kisfia], Origo, 19 February 2015. from his third marriage. His spouse was [[Klára Dobrev]], whose maternal grandfather [[Antal Apró]] was Hungary's Minister of Industry in the 1950s–60s. In May 2025, the couple announced their divorce.{{Cite web |last=Népszava |title=Minden tisztségéről lemond és visszavonul Gyurcsány Ferenc, válik Dobrev Klárától |url=https://nepszava.hu/3278913_gyurcsany-ferenc-dobrev-klara-demokratikus-koalicio-valas |access-date=2025-05-08 |website=Népszava |language=hu}} He got his nickname "Fletó"Not to be confused with the Spanish word fleto. from his elementary school Russian language teacher.[https://www.blikk.hu/aktualis/politika/majdnem-kicsaptak-a-kis-fletot-a-gimibol/rjee6dy], Blikk, 12 February 2011. ==Criticism== ===Wealth=== The origin of Gyurcsány's wealth was regularly questioned by the media and the [[Fidesz]] opposition at the time. In 2006 the weekly paper ''[[Heti Világgazdaság|HVG]]'' wrote about a biography of Gyurcsány: "[it] concludes that talent played a greater role than corruption in Gyurcsány's success. We have to question this claim. Not just because former functionaries are massively overrepresented among Gyurcsány's business partners, but also because, despite his enormous talent for business, Gyurcsány would never have got where he is today without making use of the contacts and support base of the former state party."{{cite web |url=http://hvg.hu/english/20060619gyurcsanybioeng.aspx |title=English version: Not as good as the Orban |website=HVG.hu |date=19 June 2006 |access-date=11 September 2016 |archive-date=16 January 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090116035533/http://hvg.hu/english/20060619gyurcsanybioeng.aspx |url-status=dead }} József Debreczeni, the biographer in question, originally reached the conclusion "regarding party connections and performance, the latter has been more important".{{cite book |last=Debreczeni |first=József |title= Az új miniszterelnök (The New Prime Minister) |publisher= Osiris Kiadó |year= 2006 |isbn= 963-389-844-7 |language=hu}} In 2005, MP [[Péter Szijjártó]] (of [[Fidesz]], an opposition party at that time), as the head of a committee set up to investigate the origins of Gyurcsány's wealth, stated in his report that one of Gyurcsány's companies leased the former vacation site of the Hungarian government in Balatonőszöd and rented the site back to a state-owned company so that the rent paid by the government covered exactly the leasing fee during the first two and a half years of the ten-year lease term (1994–2004).{{cite web|url=http://index.hu/politika/belfold/gya6967/ |title=Index - Belföld - Szijjártó tíz tétele Gyurcsány gazdagodásáról |website=Index.hu |date=8 November 2005 |access-date=11 September 2016}} A person named "Gyurcsányi" was mentioned by [[Attila Kulcsár]], the main defendant in a high-profile "K&H Equities" money laundering scandal in Hungary.[http://www.pestiside.hu/archives/nerves_shredded_over_scandal_evidence_shredding000808.php Nerves Shredded Over Scandal Evidence Shredding] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060508185125/http://www.pestiside.hu/archives/nerves_shredded_over_scandal_evidence_shredding000808.php |date=8 May 2006 }} Pestiside.hu The prime minister denied he had any connections with the case. ===Plagiarism controversy=== {{Main|Ferenc Gyurcsány plagiarism controversy}} In an article published on 2 April 2012, ''Pécsi Újság'' called into question whether Gyurcsány submitted a diploma thesis.{{cite web|url=http://www.pecsiujsag.hu/pecs/hir/helyi-hireink/terjed-hogy-gyurcsany-pecsi-szakdolgozata-sincs-rendben-de-a-pte-nem-adhat-ki-adatot---video|title=Pécsi Újság - Helyi híreink - Terjed, hogy Gyurcsány pécsi szakdolgozata sincs rendben, de a PTE nem adhat ki adatot - VIDEÓ|work=Pécsi Újság|access-date=14 February 2015}} István Geresdi, [[Dean (education)|Dean]] of the Faculty of Sciences at the [[University of Pécs]] told ''Pécsi Újság'' that they were unable to find Gyurcsány's diploma thesis. He further added that Gyurcsány's thesis was the only missing work from that time period. On 3 April Gyurcsány published a page from his course record book that stated that he submitted and defended a college thesis.{{Cite web |url=http://www.facebook.com/photo.php?fbid=379552925410509 |title=Ez is egy szakdolgozat értékelés | Facebook |website=[[Facebook]] |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120731054747/http://www.facebook.com/photo.php?fbid=379552925410509 |archive-date=31 July 2012 |url-status=dead}} He also stated that he did not know where his own copy of his thesis was, but he would make efforts to locate and publish it. After two weeks, on 13 April he announced that he failed to find his copy of the thesis.{{cite web|url=https://www.facebook.com/note.php?note_id=385994531433015|title=Érettségim sincs?|website=[[Facebook]]|access-date=14 February 2015}} On 27 April ''[[Hír TV]]'', a government-leaning television channel announced that they have found evidence that Szabolcs Rozs, who was Gyurcsány's brother-in-law in 1984, submitted a college thesis at the same college and department as Gyurcsány, with a title identical to Gyurcsány's work, in 1980.{{cite web|url=http://mno.hu/belfold/plagium-gyanujaban-gyurcsany-1071944|title=Plágium gyanújában Gyurcsány|work=mno.hu|access-date=14 February 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150214235201/http://mno.hu/belfold/plagium-gyanujaban-gyurcsany-1071944|archive-date=14 February 2015|url-status=dead}} Three days later, on 30 April ''Hír Tv'' announced that they have located and compared the reviews of both Rozs's and Gyurcsány's work, and found that based on the common errors and omissions, the two texts are likely to be identical, supporting the allegations of plagiarism.{{cite web|url=http://mno.hu/belfold/bebukta-gyurcsany-ferenc-plagizalt-video-1072270|title=Bebukta: Gyurcsány Ferenc plagizált + Videó|work=mno.hu|access-date=14 February 2015|archive-date=15 December 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181215175115/https://mno.hu/belfold/bebukta-gyurcsany-ferenc-plagizalt-video-1072270|url-status=dead}} ===Other=== On 2 September 2004, he said in the [[m1 (TV channel)|Hungarian national television]]: "Who has a two-room-apartment, would in general deserve three; who has three, four; who has four, a house. Who has an eld..., olderly, elderly?... olderly ''[struggling with an unintended [[portmanteau]]]'' wife, a younger one; who has a badly behaved kid, a well-behaved. Of course, he would deserve."Original Hungarian text: ''Akinek kétszobás lakása van, az általában megérdemelne hármat, akinek három, az négyet, akinek négy, az egy családi házat. Akinek öreged..., öregecskedő, öregeskedő?... öregecskedő felesége, az fiatalabbat, akinek rendetlen gyereke, az rendesebbet. Persze, hogy megérdemelné.'' See video at [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OKM0DmfbAgk YouTube] This triggered outrage from feminist organisations, women in general, and the opposition.[http://index.hu/belfold/noi1014r/ Index.hu] – ''Felháborodtak az asszonyok Gyurcsány kijelentésén'' (Women outraged at Gyurcsány's statement) 14 September 2004.[http://index.hu/belfold/hirek/193249/ Index.hu] – ''Áder bocsánatkérésre szólítja fel Gyurcsányt'' ([[János Áder|Áder]] calls on Gyurcsány to apologize), 11 September 2004. On 2 February 2005, at the birthday party of the [[Hungarian Socialist Party]], for the sake of a joke, Gyurcsány referred to the players of the [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi national football team]] as terrorists. Later he apologized, but the kingdom recalled its ambassador from Hungary for a time.{{cite web|url=http://index.hu/politika/belfold/szaud0211/|title=Index - Belföld - Jegyzékben tiltakoznak 'a szaúdi terroristák' Gyurcsánynál|date=11 February 2005|access-date=14 February 2015}}{{cite web|url=http://www.magyarhirlap.hu/Archivum_cikk.php?cikk%3D92593%26archiv%3D1%26next%3D0 |title=Magyar Hírlap |access-date=6 April 2006 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071009035219/http://www.magyarhirlap.hu/Archivum_cikk.php?cikk=92593&archiv=1&next=0 |archive-date=9 October 2007 }} During the 2006 general election campaign, a video appeared where Gyurcsány danced as [[Hugh Grant]] in ''[[Love Actually]]''.{{cite web|url=http://home.eircom.net/content/reuters/uNews/7379070?view%3DStandard |title=Eircom net Ireland-International / Irish news headlines from leading Irish newspapers |access-date=11 February 2006 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061008230926/http://home.eircom.net/content/reuters/uNews/7379070?view=Standard |archive-date=8 October 2006 }} According to government officials, the spokesperson of the government asked Gyurcsány to dance, as they re-made most parts of the film as a special gift for the wedding of spokesman [[András Batiz]]. Opposition claimed that the video was made public on purpose, as part of the election campaign, to gain popularity for the PM among young adults. After his return to politics, Gyurcsány was at first tight-lipped on his religious affiliation, leading many to assume that he is an [[atheist]]. In an interview aired on TV2 during the 2006 parliamentary election campaign, Gyurcsány said that as a teenager, he "took part in [[confirmation]] for about two years" and even considered becoming a [[priest]]."Mokka", TV2, 26 March 2006. Since confirmation can only be taken once, some regarded this claim as a giveaway that he was not telling the truth, while others such as Catholic bishop Endre Gyulay supposed he meant he took part in preparations for a confirmation.[http://www.magyarkurir.hu/?m_op=view&id=9533&rovat=4 Napi sajtószemle], Magyar Kurír, 28 March 2006 {{in lang|hu}} In connection with the unrest fuelled by his speech, he has been criticised in ''[[The Economist]]'' for "turning a blind eye to police brutality".[http://www.economist.com/daily/columns/europeview/displaystory.cfm?story_id=8491837 Europe.view], ''[[The Economist]]'', 4 January 2007. On 13 January 2009, [[Dominique Strauss-Kahn]], the managing director of the [[International Monetary Fund]], travelled to Budapest to ask Gyurcsány about their agreement made in October, regarding the stabilization of Hungarian government spending. His legitimacy was often questioned by opposition parties based on his withholding of information about the actual budget deficit in his 2006 re-election campaign.{{cite web |title=Bizottsági vita a 2006-os államháztartási adatokról - MR1-Kossuth Rádió |url=http://www.mr1-kossuth.hu/hirek/itthon/a-ksh-adatszolgaltatasarol-targyal-a-koltsegvetesi-bizottsag.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100203205037/http://www.mr1-kossuth.hu/hirek/itthon/a-ksh-adatszolgaltatasarol-targyal-a-koltsegvetesi-bizottsag.html |archive-date=3 February 2010 |access-date=26 February 2010}} ===Russia Pipeline=== [[File:Vladimir Putin 28 February 2008-5.jpg|260px|thumb|right|Gyurcsány (left) and [[Vladimir Putin|Putin]] (right) in February 2008]] As a prime minister, Gyurcsány was said to be an advocate of the South Stream pipeline project, which is aimed to supply Russian gas directly to the EU, bypassing transit countries such as Ukraine. He signed the contract in Moscow just week before a referendum in Hungary, which showed around 80% of the votes were against the government reforms.[http://www.mn.mno.hu/portal/549993 Magyar Nemzet: "Gyurcsány elindult a schröderi úton?"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110721111804/http://www.mn.mno.hu/portal/549993 |date=21 July 2011 }}, 21 March 2008. However, the questions of the referendum (two concerning health care and one concerning education) had no relation to the issue of possible pipelines built in the country. Gyurcsány stated that it is an unlucky situation for a country to have only one supplier (Russia) of any resource, which in this particular case is natural gas.{{cite web|url=http://www.vg.hu/vallalatok/energia/bolond-az-az-orszag-amelyik-attol-boldog-hogy-egy-eladoja-van-gyurcsany-az-energiaellatasrol-188190|title="Bolond az az ország, amelyik attól boldog, hogy egy eladója van" - Gyurcsány az energiaellátásról - Energia - Vállalatok - Világgazdaság Online|work=VG|date=14 September 2007 |access-date=14 February 2015}} He said the [[South Stream]] pipeline only diversifies routes from the same source country. He also advocated the [[Nabucco pipeline]] which was planned to transfer gas from the Middle East.{{Cite web |last=MEES |title=Russia And Serbia Follow Up South Stream Agreement, Hungary Joins Gazprom Project |url=https://www.zawya.com/en/business/russia-and-serbia-follow-up-south-stream-agreement-hungary-joins-gazprom-project-t0aqjvfz |access-date=2025-06-16 |website=www.zawya.com |language=en}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Notes== {{Notelist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061008230926/http://home.eircom.net/content/reuters/uNews/7379070?view=Standard Hungarian PM appears on Internet as Hugh Grant] * [http://portal.dkp.hu/ The page of the Demokratikus Koalíció party] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190313162030/http://portal.dkp.hu/ |date=13 March 2019 }} {{in lang|hu}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20040808020503/http://www.mszp.hu/index.php?gcPage=public%2Fportretar%2FmutatPortre&fnId=13425 His biography] {{in lang|hu}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20050309142525/http://www.origo.hu/uzletinegyed/hirek/2002111541tol.html The 100 richest people in Hungary], 2002., origo.hu. ([http://www.origo.hu/uzletinegyed/hirek/20021115elso.html Original URL]) {{in lang|hu}} {{s-start}} {{s-off}} {{succession box | title = [[Government of Hungary|Minister of Youth Affairs and Sports]] | before = [[György Jánosi]] | years = 2003–2004 | after = [[Kinga Göncz]] }} {{succession box | title = [[Prime Minister of Hungary]] | before = [[Péter Medgyessy]] | years = 2004–2009 | after = [[Gordon Bajnai]] }} {{s-ppo}} {{Succession box | before = [[István Hiller]] | title = [[Hungarian Socialist Party|Chairman of the Hungarian Socialist Party]] | years = 2007–2009 | after = [[Ildikó Lendvai]] }} {{s-break}} {{s-non|reason=New political party}} {{s-ttl|title=[[Democratic Coalition (Hungary)|Chairman of the Democratic Coalition]]|years=2011–2025}} {{s-aft|after=[[Klára Dobrev]]}} {{s-par|hu}} {{s-break}} {{s-non|reason=New parliamentary group}} {{s-ttl|title=Leader of the [[Democratic Coalition (Hungary)|DK]] parliamentary group|years=2018–2025}} {{s-aft|after=[[László Sebián-Petrovszki]]}} {{s-end}} {{Navboxes top|}} {{HungarianPrimeMinisters}} {{First Gyurcsány Government}} {{navboxes bottom}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Gyurcsany, Ferenc}} [[Category:Ferenc Gyurcsány| ]] [[Category:1961 births]] [[Category:Living people]] [[Category:Democratic Coalition (Hungary) politicians]] [[Category:Hungarian businesspeople]] [[Category:Hungarian Socialist Party politicians]] [[Category:Members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2006–2010)]] [[Category:Members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2010–2014)]] [[Category:Members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2014–2018)]] [[Category:Members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2018–2022)]] [[Category:Members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2022–2026)]] [[Category:People from Pápa]] [[Category:Prime ministers of Hungary]] [[Category:Leaders of political parties in Hungary]]
contested.clean_text: {{Infobox officeholder | name = Ferenc Gyurcsány | image = Ferenc Gyurcsany 2012.jpg | caption = Gyurcsány in 2004 | office = Prime Minister of Hungary | term_start = 29 September 2004 | term_end = 14 April 2009 | president = | deputy = | predecessor = Péter Medgyessy | successor = Gordon Bajnai | office1 = President of the Democratic Coalition | term_start1 = 22 October 2011 | term_end1 = 8 May 2025 | predecessor1 = Party established | successor1 = Klára Dobrev | office2 = President of the Hungarian Socialist Party | term_start2 = 27 February 2007 | term_end2 = 5 April 2009 | predecessor2 = István Hiller | successor2 = Ildikó Lendvai | office3 = Minister of Youth Affairs and Sports | term_start3 = 19 May 2003 | term_end3 = 4 October 2004 | prime_minister3 = Péter MedgyessyHimself | deputy3 = | predecessor3 = György Jánosi | successor3 = Kinga Göncz | office4 = Member of the National Assembly | term_start4 = 16 May 2006 | term_end4 = 9 May 2025 | constituency4 = National List | birth_date = | birth_place = Pápa, Hungary | death_date = | death_place = | spouse = Beatrix Rozs (divorced) | children = 5 | parents = | party = DK (2011–2025) | other_party = | alma_mater = University of Pécs | occupation = }} Ferenc Gyurcsány{{efn|}} (born 4 June 1961) is a Hungarian entrepreneur and retired politician who served as Prime Minister of Hungary from 2004 to 2009. Prior to that, he held the position of Minister of Youth Affairs and Sports between 2003 and 2004. He was nominated as prime minister by the Hungarian Socialist Party (MSZP) on 25 August 2004, after Péter Medgyessy resigned due to a conflict with the Socialist Party's coalition partner. Gyurcsány was elected prime minister on 29 September 2004 in a parliamentary vote (197 yes votes, 12 no votes, with most of the opposition in Parliament not voting). He led his coalition to victory in the 2006 parliamentary election, securing another term as prime minister. On 24 February 2007, he was elected as the leader of the MSZP, winning 89% of the vote. On 21 March 2009, Gyurcsány announced his intention to resign as prime minister. President László Sólyom stated that instead of a short-term government ruling only until the 2010 elections, early elections should be held. On 28 March 2009 Gyurcsány resigned from his position as party chairman. A minister under Gyurcsány, Gordon Bajnai, became the nominee of MSZP for the post of prime minister in March 2009 and he became prime minister on 14 April. In October 2011, Gyurcsány and other party members quit the MSZP to establish the Democratic Coalition (DK) under his leadership. In May 2025, he announced his resignation from the leadership of the party, and also his retirement from politics. Gyurcsány was born in Pápa, Hungary, into an impoverished middle-class family as the only son of Ferenc Gyurcsány Sr. and Katalin Varga. He has an elder sister, Éva. According to contemporary police documents, Gyurcsány's father was convicted on charges of minor crimes (low value thefts and fraud) multiple times. Gyurcsány attended Apáczai Csere János High School in Budapest for two years; he then returned to Pápa, Hungary and continued his studies there, at a local grammar school. In 1979, he was admitted to the University of Pécs, where he studied as a teacher and obtained his B.Sc. in 1984, then he studied economics at the same institution, getting his degree in 1990. In 1981, he assumed function in the KISZ, the Organisation of Young Communists, where he mostly handled organizing student programs at the beginning. Between 1984 and 1988, he was the vice president of the organisation's committee in Pécs. Then between 1988 and 1989, he was the president of the central KISZ committee of universities and colleges. After the political change in 1989, he became vice-president of the organisation's short-lived quasi successor, the Hungarian Democratic Youth Association (DEMISZ). From 1990 onwards, he transferred from the public to the private sector. Gyurcsány then took the position of CEO at Altus Ltd., a holding company of which he was owner, from 1992 to 2002 and thereafter as chairman of the board. By 2002, he was listed as the fiftieth-richest person in Hungary. Gyurcsány returned to politics in 2002 as the head strategic advisor of Péter Medgyessy, the previous prime minister of Hungary. From May 2003 until September 2004 Gyurcsány was a minister responsible for sports, youth and children. He became the president of the MSZP in Győr-Moson-Sopron county in January 2004, serving until September 2004. In the summer of that same year it seemed that there were larger problems in his relationship with Prime Minister Péter Medgyessy, so he resigned as minister. In a week, problems in the coalition led to the resignation of Medgyessy, and MSZP voted Gyurcsány to become prime minister as he was acceptable for the coalition partner, Alliance of Free Democrats (SZDSZ). thumb|Gyurcsány with U.S. president [[George W. Bush in Washington, D.C., 7 October 2005]] Gyurcsány was reappointed as prime minister after the 2006 parliamentary elections, with his coalition taking 210 of the available 386 parliamentary seats, and making him the first Hungarian prime minister to keep the office after a general election since 1990. On 24 February 2007, he also became the leader of his party (being the only candidate for the post), gaining 89% of the votes. However, soon after the election victory serious financial problems arose. His government was forced to implement austerity measures to somehow manage the budget deficit, which was much higher than expected and had grown to an almost 10% of the GDP by the end of 2006. These measures were heavily criticized by both the opposition, led by Fidesz, as being too harsh on the people, and by liberal economists, for not reducing spending enough on social benefits, including pensions. On 17 September 2006, an audio recording surfaced, allegedly from a closed-door meeting of the Prime Minister's party MSZP, held on 26 May 2006, shortly after MSZP won the election. On the recording, Gyurcsány admitted "We have obviously been lying for the last one and a half to two years." ("Nyilvánvalóan végighazudtuk az utolsó másfél-két évet.") and that "We've been lying at morning, evening, and night" ("Hazudtunk reggel, éjjel, és este."). The later-formed public consensus on the infamous "Őszöd speech" is that Gyurcsány was obviously drunk at the time and that he wasn't supposed to speak on the aforementioned closed-door meeting. Despite public outrage, the Prime Minister refused to resign, and a series of demonstrations started near the Hungarian Parliament, swelling from 2,000 to about 8,000 demonstrators calling for the resignation of Gyurcsány and his government for several weeks. The Prime Minister admitted the authenticity of the recording. thumb|Gyurcsány at the Socialist Party's congress On 1 October, the governing party suffered a landslide defeat in the local municipal elections. On the eve of the elections, before the results were known, President László Sólyom gave a speech in which he said that the solution to the situation is in the hands of the majority in Parliament. As Prime Minister, Gyurcsány was a strong advocate of the South Stream pipeline project, which aimed to supply Russian gas directly to the European Union (EU), bypassing transit countries such as Ukraine. He signed the contract in Moscow just a week before the popular election in Hungary, which showed around 80% of the votes were against the government reforms. On 6 October 2006, Gyurcsány won a vote of confidence in Parliament, 207–165, with no coalition MP voting against him. The vote was public. On 21 March 2009, Gyurcsány announced his intention to resign as prime minister. President László Sólyom stated that instead of a short-term transactional government ruling only until the 2010 elections, early elections should be held. On 28 March, Gyurcsány resigned from his position as party chairman. As a leader of DK, he announced that his party would support Gordon Bajnai as a candidate for prime minister in 2014. In September 2012, the ruling Fidesz proposed a voter-registration plan, which, according to the opposition, "would have restricted the right to vote". Gyurcsány and three other members of his party participated in a week-long hunger strike against the proposal. Later, in January 2013, the Constitutional Court of Hungary struck down the new electoral law as unconstitutional; after that decision, Fidesz caucus dropped the law. Gyurcsány referred to that act as his party's success. On 14 January 2014, the Democratic Coalition and four other groups founded Unity, a political alliance with the aim of defeating Fidesz at the elections in the spring. The alliance made it into the Parliament, but only as opposition. DK won only four seats, which meant that since they were below the minimum requirements of forming a parliamentary group (five seats), its MPs (including Gyurcsány) officially count as independent politicians. On 8 April 2018, the Democratic Coalition won nine seats, creating now a political group in the National Assembly. In the 2019 European elections, his party got 16% of the vote and became the largest opposition party until the 2024 European elections. On 8 May 2025, Gyurcsány announced that he would resign from the leadership of the Democratic Coalition and from his seat in parliament, and would retire from politics. from his third marriage. His spouse was Klára Dobrev, whose maternal grandfather Antal Apró was Hungary's Minister of Industry in the 1950s–60s. In May 2025, the couple announced their divorce. He got his nickname "Fletó" from his elementary school Russian language teacher. The origin of Gyurcsány's wealth was regularly questioned by the media and the Fidesz opposition at the time. In 2006 the weekly paper HVG wrote about a biography of Gyurcsány: "[it] concludes that talent played a greater role than corruption in Gyurcsány's success. We have to question this claim. Not just because former functionaries are massively overrepresented among Gyurcsány's business partners, but also because, despite his enormous talent for business, Gyurcsány would never have got where he is today without making use of the contacts and support base of the former state party." József Debreczeni, the biographer in question, originally reached the conclusion "regarding party connections and performance, the latter has been more important". In 2005, MP Péter Szijjártó (of Fidesz, an opposition party at that time), as the head of a committee set up to investigate the origins of Gyurcsány's wealth, stated in his report that one of Gyurcsány's companies leased the former vacation site of the Hungarian government in Balatonőszöd and rented the site back to a state-owned company so that the rent paid by the government covered exactly the leasing fee during the first two and a half years of the ten-year lease term (1994–2004). A person named "Gyurcsányi" was mentioned by Attila Kulcsár, the main defendant in a high-profile "K&H Equities" money laundering scandal in Hungary. The prime minister denied he had any connections with the case. In an article published on 2 April 2012, Pécsi Újság called into question whether Gyurcsány submitted a diploma thesis. István Geresdi, Dean of the Faculty of Sciences at the University of Pécs told Pécsi Újság that they were unable to find Gyurcsány's diploma thesis. He further added that Gyurcsány's thesis was the only missing work from that time period. On 3 April Gyurcsány published a page from his course record book that stated that he submitted and defended a college thesis. He also stated that he did not know where his own copy of his thesis was, but he would make efforts to locate and publish it. After two weeks, on 13 April he announced that he failed to find his copy of the thesis. On 27 April Hír TV, a government-leaning television channel announced that they have found evidence that Szabolcs Rozs, who was Gyurcsány's brother-in-law in 1984, submitted a college thesis at the same college and department as Gyurcsány, with a title identical to Gyurcsány's work, in 1980. Three days later, on 30 April Hír Tv announced that they have located and compared the reviews of both Rozs's and Gyurcsány's work, and found that based on the common errors and omissions, the two texts are likely to be identical, supporting the allegations of plagiarism. On 2 September 2004, he said in the Hungarian national television: "Who has a two-room-apartment, would in general deserve three; who has three, four; who has four, a house. Who has an eld..., olderly, elderly?... olderly [struggling with an unintended portmanteau] wife, a younger one; who has a badly behaved kid, a well-behaved. Of course, he would deserve." This triggered outrage from feminist organisations, women in general, and the opposition. On 2 February 2005, at the birthday party of the Hungarian Socialist Party, for the sake of a joke, Gyurcsány referred to the players of the Saudi national football team as terrorists. Later he apologized, but the kingdom recalled its ambassador from Hungary for a time. During the 2006 general election campaign, a video appeared where Gyurcsány danced as Hugh Grant in Love Actually. According to government officials, the spokesperson of the government asked Gyurcsány to dance, as they re-made most parts of the film as a special gift for the wedding of spokesman András Batiz. Opposition claimed that the video was made public on purpose, as part of the election campaign, to gain popularity for the PM among young adults. After his return to politics, Gyurcsány was at first tight-lipped on his religious affiliation, leading many to assume that he is an atheist. In an interview aired on TV2 during the 2006 parliamentary election campaign, Gyurcsány said that as a teenager, he "took part in confirmation for about two years" and even considered becoming a priest. Since confirmation can only be taken once, some regarded this claim as a giveaway that he was not telling the truth, while others such as Catholic bishop Endre Gyulay supposed he meant he took part in preparations for a confirmation. In connection with the unrest fuelled by his speech, he has been criticised in The Economist for "turning a blind eye to police brutality". On 13 January 2009, Dominique Strauss-Kahn, the managing director of the International Monetary Fund, travelled to Budapest to ask Gyurcsány about their agreement made in October, regarding the stabilization of Hungarian government spending. His legitimacy was often questioned by opposition parties based on his withholding of information about the actual budget deficit in his 2006 re-election campaign. 260px|thumb|right|Gyurcsány (left) and [[Vladimir Putin|Putin (right) in February 2008]] As a prime minister, Gyurcsány was said to be an advocate of the South Stream pipeline project, which is aimed to supply Russian gas directly to the EU, bypassing transit countries such as Ukraine. He signed the contract in Moscow just week before a referendum in Hungary, which showed around 80% of the votes were against the government reforms. However, the questions of the referendum (two concerning health care and one concerning education) had no relation to the issue of possible pipelines built in the country. Gyurcsány stated that it is an unlucky situation for a country to have only one supplier (Russia) of any resource, which in this particular case is natural gas. He said the South Stream pipeline only diversifies routes from the same source country. He also advocated the Nabucco pipeline which was planned to transfer gas from the Middle East. * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061008230926/http://home.eircom.net/content/reuters/uNews/7379070?view=Standard Hungarian PM appears on Internet as Hugh Grant] * [http://portal.dkp.hu/ The page of the Demokratikus Koalíció party] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20040808020503/http://www.mszp.hu/index.php?gcPage=public%2Fportretar%2FmutatPortre&fnId=13425 His biography] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20050309142525/http://www.origo.hu/uzletinegyed/hirek/2002111541tol.html The 100 richest people in Hungary], 2002., origo.hu. ([http://www.origo.hu/uzletinegyed/hirek/20021115elso.html Original URL]) Category:1961 births Category:Living people Category:Democratic Coalition (Hungary) politicians Category:Hungarian businesspeople Category:Hungarian Socialist Party politicians Category:Members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2006–2010) Category:Members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2010–2014) Category:Members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2014–2018) Category:Members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2018–2022) Category:Members of the National Assembly of Hungary (2022–2026) Category:People from Pápa Category:Prime ministers of Hungary Category:Leaders of political parties in Hungary

stable.title: 1998 Dublin North by-election
stable.raw_text:
{{Short description|By-election to the 28th Dáil}} {{good article}} {{Use dmy dates|date=February 2022}} {{Use Hiberno-English|date=February 2022}} {{Infobox election |election_name = 1998 Dublin North by-election |country = Ireland |type = by-election |ongoing = no |previous_election = 1997 Irish general election |previous_year = 1997 |election_date = 11 March 1998 |next_election = 2002 Irish general election |next_year = 2002 |turnout = 33,046 (50.2%) |1blank = {{nowrap|First preferences}} |2blank = Percentage |3blank = Final count |image1 =
'''Ryan'''
|nominee1 = '''[[Seán Ryan (Irish politician)|Seán Ryan]]''' |party1 = Labour Party (Ireland) |1data1 = 11,012 |2data1 = 33.3% |3data1 = '''16,896''' |image2 =
'''Kennedy '''
|nominee2 = [[Michael Kennedy (Dublin politician)|Michael Kennedy]] |party2 = Fianna Fáil |1data2 = 10,334 |2data2 = 31.3% |3data2 = 13,633 |image3 =
'''Jenkinson'''
|nominee3 = Philip Jenkinson |party3 = Fine Gael |1data3 = 3,185 |2data3 = 9.6% |3data3 = – |map_image = Dublin North Dáil Éireann constituency.png |map_size = 150px |map_caption = [[Dublin North (Dáil constituency)|Dublin North]] shown within [[County Dublin]] |title = [[Teachta Dála|TD]] |before_election = [[Ray Burke (Irish politician)|Ray Burke]] |before_party = Fianna Fáil |posttitle = [[Teachta Dála|TD]] after election |after_election = [[Seán Ryan (Irish politician)|Seán Ryan]] |after_party = Labour Party (Ireland) }} A [[Dáil by-election]] was held in the [[Dáil constituencies|constituency]] of [[Dublin North (Dáil constituency)|Dublin North]] in [[Republic of Ireland|Ireland]] on Wednesday, 11 March 1998, to fill a vacancy in the [[28th Dáil]]. It followed the resignation of [[Fianna Fáil]] [[Teachta Dála]] (TD) [[Ray Burke (Irish politician)|Ray Burke]] on 7 October 1997. The by-election was won by [[Seanad Éireann|Senator]] [[Seán Ryan (Irish politician)|Seán Ryan]] of the [[Labour Party (Ireland)|Labour Party]]. 19 candidates contested the by-election, including 9 [[Independent politician (Ireland)|independents]]. The winner was declared after 14 counts, with eight candidates getting less than 1% of the first preference vote. On the same day, a by-election took place in [[1998 Limerick East by-election|Limerick East]]; both were the final occasions in which [[Democratic Left (Ireland)|Democratic Left]] contested by-elections. Both by-elections were won by Labour candidates. == Background == [[Ray Burke (Irish politician)|Ray Burke]], a [[Fianna Fáil|Fianna Fáíl]] TD for [[Dublin North (Dáil constituency)|Dublin North]], was appointed as [[Minister for Foreign Affairs and Trade|Minister for Foreign Affairs]] following the [[1997 Irish general election|1997 general election]]; however, allegations resurfaced that Burke had received [[IR£]]80,000 from a [[property developer]].{{cite web |date=19 April 2002 |title=Introduction to second Interim Report |url=http://www.flood-tribunal.ie/images/Report.pdf |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20041224014203/http://www.flood-tribunal.ie/images/Report.pdf |archive-date=24 December 2004 |access-date=15 December 2004 |work=Flood Tribunal |pages=2–3}} While denying the allegations, Burke resigned both as a minister and as a TD on 7 October 1997.{{cite journal |title=Resignation of Member |journal=Dáil Debates |date=7 October 1997 |volume=481 |issue=1 |url=https://www.oireachtas.ie/en/debates/debate/dail/1997-10-07/2/}}{{Cite news |last=Cullen |first=Kevin |date=8 October 1997 |title=In Belfast, talks get under way Irish aide resigns on unrelated issue |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/403906076 |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Boston Globe |id={{ProQuest|403906076}} }} A 1998 article in the ''[[Irish Independent]]'' noted that Burke's brother had died a week before his resignation, describing it as a "time of deep-seated grief" for him.{{Cite web |date=12 June 1998 |title=Burke at bay |url=https://www.independent.ie/irish-news/burke-at-bay/26183682.html |access-date=5 January 2026 |website=Irish Independent|language=en}} The [[writ of election]] was moved on 17 February 1998.{{cite journal |title=Issue of Writ: Dublin North By-election |journal=Dáil Debates |date=17 February 1998 |volume=487 |issue=3 |url=https://www.oireachtas.ie/en/debates/debate/dail/1998-02-17/14/}}{{Cite news |date=20 February 1998 |title=Notice of Dáil Election Constituency of Dublin North |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/irish-independent-notice-of-dil-electio/176275856/ |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Irish Independent|pages=26}} == Candidate selection == === Democratic Left === This by-election was the first and only time [[Democratic Left (Ireland)|Democratic Left]] ran a candidate in this constituency.{{Cite web |last=O'Regan |first=Michael |date=25 January 1999 |title=Labour and DL formally merge at the Rotunda |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/news/labour-and-dl-formally-merge-at-the-rotunda-1.1257660 |access-date=5 January 2026 |website=The Irish Times |language=en}} Their candidate, language school co-owner Joe Holohan, had contested the constituency at the [[1981 Irish general election|1981 general election]] for the [[Labour Party (Ireland)|Labour Party]], but later became a founding member of Democratic Left.{{Cite news |last=O'Regan |first=Michael |date=23 February 1998 |title=Voters are apathetic in fiefdom that burke long held for FF as the campaign intensifies, candidates in the Dublin North by-election are finding it difficult to interest voters, writes Michael O'Regan |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/310352774 |access-date=7 July 2025 |newspaper=The Irish Times |id={{ProQuest|310352774}} }} === Fianna Fáil === Initial reports suggested that Fianna Fáil were struggling to find a candidate to defend the seat. Candidates who declined to seek the nomination included Graham Turley, the widower of murdered crime reporter [[Veronica Guerin]],{{Cite news |date=1997-10-26 |title=No thanks |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/sunday-independent-dublin-ed-no-thank/176273337/ |access-date=2025-07-09 |work=Sunday Independent (Dublin ed.) |pages=3}} and former [[Dublin GAA]] goalkeeper [[John O'Leary (Gaelic footballer)|John O'Leary]].{{Cite news |last=Moran |first=John |date=13 December 1997 |title=O'Leary joins Carr as Dublin selector |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/sport/o-leary-joins-carr-as-dublin-selector-1.137149 |access-date=9 July 2025 |newspaper=The Irish Times |language=en}} Other speculated candidates included Eimear Mulhearn, daughter of former Taoiseach [[Charles Haughey]],{{Cite news |date=16 November 1997 |title=Inside Track |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/sunday-independent-dublin-ed-inside-t/176274126/ |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Sunday Independent (Dublin ed.) |pages=17}} and Amy Ahern, niece of Taoiseach [[Bertie Ahern]]. On 15 December 1997, county councillor [[Michael Kennedy (Dublin politician)|Michael Kennedy]] won the party's nomination, defeating nurse Catherine Clarke and barrister Eoin Martin.{{Cite news |date=15 December 1997 |title=Fingal councillor to stand for FF in poll |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/news/fingal-councillor-to-stand-for-ff-in-poll-1.137438 |access-date=9 July 2025 |newspaper=The Irish Times |language=en}} === Fine Gael === Five candidates sought the [[Fine Gael]] nomination; Lusk-based county councillor Philip Jenkinson, vice chairman of [[Young Fine Gael]] Sean Dolphin, businessman Michael Brannigan, Niall Atkins and Steve McCullough.{{Cite news |last=O'Connor |first=Kevin |date=16 November 1997 |title=Candidate selection |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/sunday-independent-dublin-ed-candidat/176273745/ |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Sunday Independent (Dublin ed.) |pages=3}}{{Cite news |last=McKenna |first=Gene |date=10 December 1997 |title=Blow to FF by-election chances as ex-Dubs star rules out running |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/irish-independent-blow-to-ff-by-election/176274577/ |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Irish Independent|pages=8}} On 11 December, the party announced Jenkinson as their selected candidate for the by-election.{{Cite news |date=12 December 1997 |title=FG selects by-election candidate |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/news/fg-selects-by-election-candidate-1.136393 |access-date=9 July 2025 |newspaper=The Irish Times |language=en}} === Green Party === The [[Green Party (Ireland)|Green Party]], who had held their seat in this constituency narrowly at the last general election, nominated healthcare worker Paul Martin from [[Portrane]]. === Labour Party === The Labour Party nominated Senator [[Seán Ryan (Irish politician)|Seán Ryan]], a former TD for the constituency who had lost his seat at the 1997 general election. After losing his seat, Ryan ran for a seat in the Seanad and was elected.{{Cite web |last=O'Regan |first=Michael |last2=Walsh |first2=Jimmy |date=8 August 1997 |title=Hanafin returns to the Seanad with hopes to overturn divorce decision |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/news/hanafin-returns-to-the-seanad-with-hopes-to-overturn-divorce-decision-1.94947 |access-date=5 January 2026 |website=The Irish Times |language=en}} === Progressive Democrats === Finian Fallon, who unsuccessfully contested the constituency at the 1997 general election for the [[Progressive Democrats]], was the party's nominee at this election. The son of murdered [[Garda Síochána|garda]] [[Richard Fallon (police officer)|Richard Fallon]],{{Cite news |last=Dowling |first=Brian |date=9 March 1998 |title=Party leaders staking all on two-horse race |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/irish-independent-party-leaders-staking/176279096/ |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Irish Independent|pages=5}} Fallon rejected calls for a voting pact between the Progressive Democrats and Fianna Fáil. His director for the election was former Fianna Fáil minister [[Martin O'Donoghue]].{{Cite news |last=O'Regan |first=Michael |date=13 March 1998 |title=Ryan win puts the smile back on Quinn's face as FF sweats out the count |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/310399316 |access-date=9 July 2025 |newspaper=The Irish Times |id={{ProQuest|310399316}} }} === Sinn Féin === [[Paul Donnelly (politician)|Paul Donnelly]] contested this election for [[Sinn Féin]]. === Socialist Party === On 30 October 1997, the [[Socialist Party (Ireland)|Socialist Party]] selected [[Clare Daly]] to be their candidate in the by-election. 29-year-old Daly had unsuccessfully contested the constituency at the 1997 general election, receiving 3000 votes. She had previously been president of [[Dublin City University Students' Union]] and a coordinator in the anti-water charge movement in Dublin North.{{Cite news |date=30 October 1997 |title=Socialist Party selects candidate |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/news/socialist-party-selects-candidate-1.120842 |access-date=9 July 2025 |newspaper=The Irish Times |language=en}} === Independents and others === Gertie Shields, founder of Mothers Against Drink Driving and independent member of Balbriggan Town Commission, declared that she would contest the election in December 1997.{{Cite news |date=6 December 1997 |title=MADD founder may seek election |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/irish-independent-madd-founder-may-seek/176274314/ |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Irish Independent|pages=6}} Single-issue candidate Elaine Rooney from Portrane ran as an independent against the [[Health board (Ireland)|Eastern Health Board's]] planned special care unit at [[St. Ita's Hospital]], while another, Rena Condrot Ruigrok, ran in support of the closure of Balleally dump.{{Cite news |last=Dillon |first=Willie |date=4 March 1998 |title=Voters raging on the roads or down in the dumps |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/irish-independent-voters-raging-on-the-r/176277567/ |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Irish Independent|pages=6}} Other candidates from inside the constituency included Angela Keaveney from [[Portmarnock]] for the [[Christian Solidarity Party]], independent Ciaran Goulding from [[Lusk, County Dublin|Lusk]], and independent Alan Nagle from Portmarnock.{{Cite news |date=3 March 1998 |title=19 by-election runners for the one precious seat |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/irish-independent-19-by-election-runners/176277081/ |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Irish Independent|pages=31}} A number of other candidates from outside the constituency's boundaries sought election; Noel O'Neill from [[Drogheda]] ran for the [[Natural Law Party (Ireland)|Natural Law Party]], while independents Benny Cooney from [[Athlone]], Peter Farrelly from Drogheda, independent [[Unionism in Ireland|Unionist]] John McDonald from [[Newtownabbey]], [[Northern Ireland]]{{Cite news |last=McManus |first=John |date=8 March 1998 |title=Unionist stands in Dublin poll |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/320624916 |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Sunday Times |pages=5 |id={{ProQuest|320624916}} }} and perennial candidate [[Perennial candidate#Ireland|Jim Tallon]] from [[Arklow]] also ran. == Campaign == Early reports suggested that Fianna Fáil anticipated a difficult election campaign, with Taoiseach Bertie Ahern admitting the party faced an "uphill battle" to keep the Dublin North seat.{{Cite news |last=Dowling |first=Brian |last2=McKenna |first2=Gene |date=12 February 1998 |title=Coalition fears double by-election defeat looming |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/irish-independent-coalition-fears-double/188228023/ |access-date=5 January 2026 |work=Irish Independent|pages=1}} Combined with the [[1998 Limerick East by-election|by-election in Limerick East]] held on the same day, this put the government at risk of a one-seat majority if they lost both, a particular concern given the government relied on three independent TDs supporting them.{{Cite news |last=Brennock |first=Mark |date=17 February 1998 |title=Date for elections will be set today |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/news/date-for-elections-will-be-set-today-1.136390 |access-date=9 July 2025 |newspaper=The Irish Times |language=en}} During the campaign, Socialist Party candidate Clare Daly applied for an injunction withdrawing the [[Fingal Independent]] newspaper from sale, alleging that the paper had published claims to damage her chances of being elected. The paper had published a claim that Daly called for her transfers to be given to Seán Ryan, a statement Daly denied making.{{Cite news |last=Maddock |first=John |date=7 March 1998 |title=Candidate takes battle for by-election to court |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/irish-independent-candidate-takes-battle/176276939/ |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Irish Independent|pages=8}} A major issue that impacted the campaign was the dump at Balleally, due to be closed the previous November until the local council vowed instead to expand it. In November 1997, 400 people had marched through Dublin city calling for the dump in Balleally to be closed, and threatened to run their own independent candidate in the election,{{Cite news |last=McDonald |first=Frank |date=20 November 1997 |title=Protest at plans to extend dump |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/news/protest-at-plans-to-extend-dump-1.128404 |access-date=9 July 2025 |newspaper=The Irish Times |language=en}} later opting to run Rena Condrot Ruigrok as this candidate. Other campaign issues included traffic issues around [[Swords, County Dublin|Swords]] and [[Dublin Airport]], a trade dispute involving [[Ryanair]] employees, school funding in the constituency, and a threatened abolition of [[Duty-free shop|duty-free]] sales at Dublin Airport placing 300 [[DAA (company)|Aer Rianta]] employees at risk of redundancy. === Predictions and polls === The Labour Party candidate Seán Ryan was initially viewed as the favourite, particularly due to name recognition, with bookmaker [[Paddy Power]] giving odds on 2 March of 1/2 for Ryan, and 11/8 for Michael Kennedy of Fianna Fáil. By 9 March, these odds had shifted to 1/5 for Ryan, 11/4 for Kennedy and 40/1 for Jenkinson.{{Cite news |last=Dowling |first=Brian |date=9 March 1998 |title=Labour poll surge puts FF majority in the balance |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/irish-independent-labour-poll-surge-puts/176279325/ |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Irish Independent|pages=1}} Two days prior to the election, Brian Dowling of the ''[[Irish Independent]]'' predicted Ryan to win the seat, though for it to go "down to the wire". {| class="wikitable sortable collapsible" style="text-align:center;font-size:95%;" |- style="height:42px;" !   }} {{STV Election box end2 |numcounts = 14 |electorate = 65,891 |valid = 33,046 |spoilt = |quota = 16,524 |turnout = 50.2% }} === Reactions === The election was seen as a blow for Fianna Fáil and a disappointing one for Fine Gael, the Green Party and Democratic Left,{{Cite news |last=O'Regan |first=Michael |date=13 March 1998 |title=Defeat not due to swing away from party, says FF director of elections |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/310399250 |archive-date= |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Irish Times |id={{ProQuest|310399250}} }} though a positive election for the Socialist Party and Sinn Féin.{{Cite news |last=Coghlan |first=Denis |date=13 March 1998 |title=Ahern comes home to face the music |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/310290461 |access-date=9 July 2025 |work=Irish Times |id={{ProQuest|310290461}} }} Ryan described the result as "vindication of my work over the years in the constituency". Fianna Fáil's director for the by-election, [[Dermot Ahern]] (no relation to partie leader Bertie),{{Cite web |last=Hennessy |first=Mark |date=11 April 2007 |title=The Namesake |url=https://www.politico.eu/article/the-namesake/ |access-date=5 January 2026 |website=POLITICO |language=}} attributed the party's defeat to several factors including Fianna Fáil's status as a government party and the fragmentation of votes due to the number of candidates, denying that it was a swing away from the party. After a disappointing result, Fine Gael deputy leader and TD for Dublin North [[Nora Owen]] commented that the party "were battling against the odds from day one". == Later careers == Seán Ryan remained a TD until the [[2007 Irish general election|2007 general election]], when he retired.{{Cite web |last=Murphy |first=Hubert |date=9 June 2005 |title=Sean Ryan set to step down at next election |url=https://www.independent.ie/regionals/dublin/fingal/sean-ryan-set-to-step-down-at-next-election/27769349.html |access-date=10 July 2025 |website=Irish Independent|language=en}} His brother [[Brendan Ryan (Dublin politician)|Brendan]] attempted to retain his seat for the Labour Party, but was unsuccessful, though he later served as a TD from 2011{{Cite news |last=O'Halloran |first=Marie |date=29 February 2016 |title=Profile: Brendan Ryan (Lab) |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/news/politics/oireachtas/profile-brendan-ryan-lab-1.2554139 |access-date=10 July 2025 |newspaper=The Irish Times |language=en}} until 2020.{{Cite web |last=McConnell |first=Daniel |date=8 January 2020 |title=Veteran TD Brendan Ryan to stand down from Labour ticket |url=https://www.irishexaminer.com/news/arid-30974362.html |access-date=10 July 2025 |website=Irish Examiner |language=en}} [[Michael Kennedy (Dublin politician)|Michael Kennedy]] would go on to serve as a [[Teachta Dála|TD]] for the constituency from 2007 to 2011.{{Cite web |date=2 March 2011 |title=Kennedy bares brunt of anger |url=https://www.independent.ie/regionals/dublin/fingal/kennedy-bares-brunt-of-anger/27808448.html |access-date=10 July 2025 |website=Irish Independent|language=en}} [[Clare Daly]] would go on to serve as a [[Teachta Dála|TD]] for this constituency from 2011 until 2016, then for its successor constituency [[Dublin Fingal]] from 2016 until her election as an [[Member of the European Parliament|MEP]], serving in that role from 2019 until 2024.{{Cite web |last=Bolton |first=Michael |date=24 October 2024 |title=Clare Daly to run in general election in Dublin Central |url=https://www.breakingnews.ie/ireland/clare-daly-to-run-in-general-election-in-dublin-central-1687025.html |access-date=10 July 2025 |website=BreakingNews.ie |language=en}} [[Paul Donnelly (politician)|Paul Donnelly]] was elected as a TD in 2020 in [[Dublin West]], and was re-elected in 2024.{{Cite news |last=Pollak |first=Sorcha |date=30 November 2024 |title=Dublin West: Paul Donnelly (SF) |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/politics/2024/11/30/dublin-west-paul-donnelly-sf/ |access-date=10 July 2025 |newspaper=The Irish Times |language=en}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Dáil stable.clean_text: {{Infobox election |election_name = 1998 Dublin North by-election |country = Ireland |type = by-election |ongoing = no |previous_election = 1997 Irish general election |previous_year = 1997 |election_date = 11 March 1998 |next_election = 2002 Irish general election |next_year = 2002 |turnout = 33,046 (50.2%) |1blank = |2blank = Percentage |3blank = Final count |image1 = Ryan |nominee1 = Seán Ryan |party1 = Labour Party (Ireland) |1data1 = 11,012 |2data1 = 33.3% |3data1 = 16,896 |image2 = Kennedy |nominee2 = Michael Kennedy |party2 = Fianna Fáil |1data2 = 10,334 |2data2 = 31.3% |3data2 = 13,633 |image3 = Jenkinson |nominee3 = Philip Jenkinson |party3 = Fine Gael |1data3 = 3,185 |2data3 = 9.6% |3data3 = – |map_image = Dublin North Dáil Éireann constituency.png |map_size = 150px |map_caption = Dublin North shown within County Dublin |title = TD |before_election = Ray Burke |before_party = Fianna Fáil |posttitle = TD after election |after_election = Seán Ryan |after_party = Labour Party (Ireland) }} A Dáil by-election was held in the constituency of Dublin North in Ireland on Wednesday, 11 March 1998, to fill a vacancy in the 28th Dáil. It followed the resignation of Fianna Fáil Teachta Dála (TD) Ray Burke on 7 October 1997. The by-election was won by Senator Seán Ryan of the Labour Party. 19 candidates contested the by-election, including 9 independents. The winner was declared after 14 counts, with eight candidates getting less than 1% of the first preference vote. On the same day, a by-election took place in Limerick East; both were the final occasions in which Democratic Left contested by-elections. Both by-elections were won by Labour candidates. Ray Burke, a Fianna Fáíl TD for Dublin North, was appointed as Minister for Foreign Affairs following the 1997 general election; however, allegations resurfaced that Burke had received IR£80,000 from a property developer. While denying the allegations, Burke resigned both as a minister and as a TD on 7 October 1997. A 1998 article in the Irish Independent noted that Burke's brother had died a week before his resignation, describing it as a "time of deep-seated grief" for him. The writ of election was moved on 17 February 1998. This by-election was the first and only time Democratic Left ran a candidate in this constituency. Their candidate, language school co-owner Joe Holohan, had contested the constituency at the 1981 general election for the Labour Party, but later became a founding member of Democratic Left. Initial reports suggested that Fianna Fáil were struggling to find a candidate to defend the seat. Candidates who declined to seek the nomination included Graham Turley, the widower of murdered crime reporter Veronica Guerin, and former Dublin GAA goalkeeper John O'Leary. Other speculated candidates included Eimear Mulhearn, daughter of former Taoiseach Charles Haughey, and Amy Ahern, niece of Taoiseach Bertie Ahern. Five candidates sought the Fine Gael nomination; Lusk-based county councillor Philip Jenkinson, vice chairman of Young Fine Gael Sean Dolphin, businessman Michael Brannigan, Niall Atkins and Steve McCullough. On 11 December, the party announced Jenkinson as their selected candidate for the by-election. The Green Party, who had held their seat in this constituency narrowly at the last general election, nominated healthcare worker Paul Martin from Portrane. Finian Fallon, who unsuccessfully contested the constituency at the 1997 general election for the Progressive Democrats, was the party's nominee at this election. The son of murdered garda Richard Fallon, Fallon rejected calls for a voting pact between the Progressive Democrats and Fianna Fáil. Paul Donnelly contested this election for Sinn Féin. Gertie Shields, founder of Mothers Against Drink Driving and independent member of Balbriggan Town Commission, declared that she would contest the election in December 1997. Single-issue candidate Elaine Rooney from Portrane ran as an independent against the Eastern Health Board's planned special care unit at St. Ita's Hospital, Other candidates from inside the constituency included Angela Keaveney from Portmarnock for the Christian Solidarity Party, independent Ciaran Goulding from Lusk, and independent Alan Nagle from Portmarnock. A number of other candidates from outside the constituency's boundaries sought election; Noel O'Neill from Drogheda ran for the Natural Law Party, while independents Benny Cooney from Athlone, Peter Farrelly from Drogheda, independent Unionist John McDonald from Newtownabbey, Northern Ireland and perennial candidate Jim Tallon from Arklow also ran. Combined with the by-election in Limerick East held on the same day, this put the government at risk of a one-seat majority if they lost both, a particular concern given the government relied on three independent TDs supporting them. During the campaign, Socialist Party candidate Clare Daly applied for an injunction withdrawing the Fingal Independent newspaper from sale, alleging that the paper had published claims to damage her chances of being elected. The paper had published a claim that Daly called for her transfers to be given to Seán Ryan, a statement Daly denied making. A major issue that impacted the campaign was the dump at Balleally, due to be closed the previous November until the local council vowed instead to expand it. later opting to run Rena Condrot Ruigrok as this candidate. Two days prior to the election, Brian Dowling of the Irish Independent predicted Ryan to win the seat, though for it to go "down to the wire". |400 |29% |style="background:#F5C3C2;" |31% |17% |7% |6% |5% |2% |0.75% |0.75% |0.75% | |2% |- |26 February 1988 | data-sort-value="MRC" |MRC |Unknown | style="background:#D9E9F4" |21% |16% |6% |5% | | | | | | |46% |6% |} Ryan topped the poll on the first count and was elected on the fourteenth count, following the redistribution of Philip Jenkinson's votes. The Labour Party won both by-elections held on this day. {{STV Election box begin2 |title = 1998 Dublin North by-election |numcounts = 14 }} The election was seen as a blow for Fianna Fáil and a disappointing one for Fine Gael, the Green Party and Democratic Left, though a positive election for the Socialist Party Ryan described the result as "vindication of my work over the years in the constituency". Fianna Fáil's director for the by-election, Dermot Ahern (no relation to partie leader Bertie), attributed the party's defeat to several factors including Fianna Fáil's status as a government party and the fragmentation of votes due to the number of candidates, denying that it was a swing away from the party. After a disappointing result, Fine Gael deputy leader and TD for Dublin North Nora Owen commented that the party "were battling against the odds from day one". His brother Brendan attempted to retain his seat for the Labour Party, but was unsuccessful, though he later served as a TD from 2011 until 2020. Michael Kennedy would go on to serve as a TD for the constituency from 2007 to 2011. Clare Daly would go on to serve as a TD for this constituency from 2011 until 2016, then for its successor constituency Dublin Fingal from 2016 until her election as an MEP, serving in that role from 2019 until 2024. Paul Donnelly was elected as a TD in 2020 in Dublin West, and was re-elected in 2024.
